Related Work

Vision-and-language navigation. Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) requires an agent to find the routes to the desired target based on instructions Thomason et al., 2020;Nguyen and Daumé III, 2019;Chen et al., 2019;Krantz et al., 2020). Specifically, there are two key challenges in VLN: grounding the natural language instruction to visual environments and generalizing to unseen environments. Early VLN datasets and protocols covered R2R, CVDN, Touchdown, and RxR (Anderson et al., 2018;Thomason et al., 2020;Chen et al., 2019;Ku et al., 2020), with variants such as R4R and NDH focusing on longer trajectories and dialog grounding. To address the first challenge, one line of research in VLN utilizes carefully designed cross-modal attention modules (Wang et al., , 2019aTan et al., 2019;Landi et al., 2019;Xia et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2020b,a;Zhu et al., 2020;Zhu et al., 2021;, progress monitor modules (Ma et al., 2019b,a;Ke et al., 2019), and object-action aware modules (Qi et al., 2020a). Cross-modal attention architectures have included co-attention, gated attention, and transformer-based fusion across panoramic viewpoints (Wang et al., 2019a;Tan et al., 2019;Landi et al., 2019;Xia et al., 2020;Zhu et al., 2020). Progress monitoring and self-critique mechanisms appeared in auxiliary training losses, imitation learning, and backtracking controllers (Ma et al., 2019a;Ma et al., 2019b;Ke et al., 2019;Zhu et al., 2021). Object-centric grounding leveraged detectors and attribute predictors for instruction phrases such as colors, categories, and spatial prepositions (Qi et al., 2020a;Wang et al., 2020a;Huang et al., 2019b;Li et al., 2019). Another line of research improves vision and language co-grounding by improving vision and language representations with pre-training techniques (Li et al., 2019;Huang et al., 2019b;Hong et al., 2021). Pre-training for VLN drew on web-scale image–text corpora, large-scale action-trajectory pairs, and masked language modeling objectives (Li et al., 2019;Huang et al., 2019b;Hong et al., 2021;Zhu et al., 2020). Li et al. (2019) directly adopts pre-trained BERT for encoding instructions,  and Hong et al. (2021) learn from a large amount of image-textaction triplets,  learns from large amount of text-image pairs from the web, and Huang et al. (2019b) transfers language and visual representation to in-domain representation with auxiliary tasks. Different from them, we utilize the visually-aligned multilingual instructions to learn a cross-lingual language representation that inherently captures visual semantics underlying the instruction.

Multiple methods have been proposed to encourage generalization to unseen environments during training (Zhang et al., 2020;Tan et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020c;Fu et al., 2020). Generalization techniques included environment dropout, adversarial path sampling, curriculum schedules, and domain-adversarial regularization (Tan et al., 2019;Fu et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2020c;Zhang et al., 2020). Instruction generation and data augmentation employed speaker models, paraphrasing, and back-translation on R2R/RxR splits (Tan et al., 2019;Zhang et al., 2020;Huang et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2019b). Evaluation protocols reported success rate, SPL, navigation error, and coverage-weighted shortest path metrics across seen and unseen environments (Nguyen and Daumé III, 2019;Thomason et al., 2020;Krantz et al., 2020;Chen et al., 2019). Zhang et al. (2020) demonstrates that it is the low-level appearance information that causes the large performance gap between seen and unseen environments. Tan et al. (2019) proposes to use environment dropout on visual features to create new environments and Fu et al. (2020) utilizes adversarial path sampling to encourage generalization. However, both of these methods rely on a speaker module to generate synthetic training data and can be considered as data augmentation methods, which are complementary to our proposed environment-agnostic visual representation. The closest work to ours is Wang et al. (2020c), where they proposes to pair an environment classifier with gradient reversal layer to learn an environment-agnostic representation. However, they only consider one single environment when learning the visual representation for a given path (i.e., given one path and predict its environment). In our environment-agnostic representation learning, we explore the connections between multiple environments (i.e., maximize the similarity between paths from different environments). Vision-and-language with multilinguality. There has been growing interest in combining vision and language for tasks such as visual-guided machine translation (Sigurdsson et al., 2020;Surís et al., 2020;Huang et al., 2020), multi-lingual visual question answering (Gao et al., 2015;Gupta et al., 2020;Shimizu et al., 2018), multi-lingual image captioning (Gu et al., 2018;Lan et al., 2017), multilingual video captioning (Wang et al., 2019b), and multi-lingual image-sentence retrieval Burns et al., 2020). Multilingual multimodal learning encompassed visual question answering, image captioning, and image–sentence retrieval across diverse language pairs (Gao et al., 2015;Gu et al., 2018;Lan et al., 2017;Gupta et al., 2020;Burns et al., 2020). In this paper, we work on multi-lingual vision-and-language navigation. We use vision (i.e., navigation path) as a bridge between multi-lingual instructions and learn a crosslingual representation that captures visual concepts. Furthermore, our approach also use language as a bridge between different visual environments to learn an environment-agnostic visual representation. Multilingual VLN resources have referenced machine translation pipelines, cross-lingual embeddings, and code-switching corpora in studies of VGM, M-VQA, and multilingual captioning (Sigurdsson et al., 2020;Surís et al., 2020;Shimizu et al., 2018;Gu et al., 2018;Lan et al., 2017).

Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Dense retrievers based on DPR, ANCE, ColBERT, and REALM (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2021; Khattab and Zaharia, 2020; Guu et al., 2020) have been widely studied alongside sparse methods such as BM25 and SPLADE (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009; Formal et al., 2021). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b). Popular ODQA benchmarks include Natural Questions, TriviaQA, WebQuestions, and CuratedTrec (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Joshi et al., 2017; Berant et al., 2013; Baudiš and Šedivý, 2015). Common evaluation metrics for ODQA include exact match, F1, Rouge-L, and recall@k (Rajpurkar et al., 2016; Lin, 2004; Manning et al., 2008; Chen et al., 2017). Retrieval corpora span Wikipedia, CC-News, Google NQ passages, and MS MARCO documents (Devlin et al., 2019; Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Ng et al., 2019; Nguyen et al., 2016). Distant supervision, negative mining, multi-task learning, and knowledge distillation have all been studied for ODQA (Mintz et al., 2009; Xiong et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2019; Hinton et al., 2015).

Generative Readers

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework.  and Lewis et al. (2020a) concatenate the given question with top retrieved passages and feed the concatenation to the BART model (Lewis et al., 2019). Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively. Pre-trained encoder-decoder models such as BART, T5, PEGASUS, and UL2 (Lewis et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020; Tay et al., 2022) are commonly adopted as backbones for open-domain generation. Approaches that aggregate evidence across passages include RAG, GENRE, FiD, and REALM-based generators (Lewis et al., 2020b; De Cao et al., 2021; Izacard and Grave, 2020b; Guu et al., 2020). Related knowledge-intensive tasks such as fact verification, slot filling, entity linking, and open-domain summarization have adopted similar retrieval-generation paradigms (Thorne et al., 2018; Petroni et al., 2019; Logeswaran et al., 2019; Chen and Bansal, 2018).

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. It has been frequently used in natural language tasks like summarization (Gu et al., 2016;See et al.,  Gehrmann et al., 2018) and neural machine translation (Luong et al., 2014;Gu et al., 2017), but its application to ODQA has been less explored. Copy-augmented architectures like CopyNet, pointer-softmax, PGNet, and hybrid transformers have been explored in QA and summarization (Gu et al., 2016; Gulcehre et al., 2016; See et al., 2017; Gehrmann et al., 2018). Work on multi-hop reasoning includes HotpotQA, QAngaroo, ComplexWebQuestions, and MuSiQue (Yang et al., 2018; Welbl et al., 2018; Talmor and Berant, 2018; Trivedi et al., 2021).

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. After the two-stage architecture became standard, dense retrieval approaches such as DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020), ORQA (Lee et al., 2019), REALM (Guu et al., 2020), and ANCE (Xiong et al., 2021) have been widely adopted alongside BM25 (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009). The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. Common ODQA benchmarks include Natural Questions (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019), TriviaQA (Joshi et al., 2017), WebQuestions (Berant et al., 2013), and CuratedTREC (Baudiš and Šedivý, 2015). For multi-hop QA, HotpotQA (Yang et al., 2018), QAngaroo/WikiHop (Welbl et al., 2018), MuSiQue (Trivedi et al., 2022), and 2WikiMultihopQA (Ho et al., 2020) are commonly used. Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b). Reader architectures include BiDAF (Seo et al., 2017), QANet (Yu et al., 2018), XLNet-based readers (Yang et al., 2019), and ELECTRA-based readers (Clark et al., 2020) in addition to BERT and T5. Training strategies for dense retrievers include in-batch negatives (Karpukhin et al., 2020), hard negative mining (Xiong et al., 2021), knowledge distillation from cross-encoders (Hofstätter et al., 2020), and self-training with pseudo labels (Izacard and Grave, 2021). Passage selection and evidence aggregation methods include TF-IDF ranking (Chen et al., 2017), multi-vector encoders like ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020), sentence-level reranking with cross-encoders (Nogueira and Cho, 2019), and graph-based aggregation across documents (De Cao et al., 2021).

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework. Sequence-to-sequence readers such as BART (Lewis et al., 2019), T5 (Raffel et al., 2019), PEGASUS (Zhang et al., 2020), and UL2 (Tay et al., 2023) are frequently employed in open-domain settings.  and Lewis et al. Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively. Retrieval-augmented frameworks include RAG (Lewis et al., 2020a), REALM (Guu et al., 2020), RETRO (Borgeaud et al., 2022), and Atlas (Izacard et al., 2022). Decoding and calibration techniques such as nucleus sampling (Holtzman et al., 2020), constrained beam search (Anderson et al., 2017), temperature scaling (Guo et al., 2017), and calibrated confidence estimation for QA (Kamath et al., 2020) have been investigated.

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. It has been frequently used in natural language tasks like summarization (Gu et al., 2016;See et al.,  Gehrmann et al., 2018) and neural machine translation (Luong et al., 2014;Gu et al., 2017), but its application to ODQA has been less explored. Copy and pointer mechanisms have been explored in question answering by Pointer Sentinel Mixture Models (Merity et al., 2017), QANet with pointer layers (Yu et al., 2018), pointer networks for span selection (Vinyals et al., 2015), and hybrid extractive–abstractive readers (Dong et al., 2019).

Introduction

Few-shot learning is the problem of learning classifiers with only a few training examples. Zero-shot learning (Larochelle et al., 2008), also known as dataless classification (Chang et al., 2008), is the extreme case, in which no labeled data is used. For text data, this is usually accomplished by representing the labels of the task in a textual form, which can either be the name of the label or a concise textual description. Benchmark corpora such as AG News (Zhang et al., 2015), DBPedia (Zhang et al., 2015), Yahoo Answers (Zhang et al., 2015), Yelp Reviews (Yelp, 2015), and IMDB (Maas et al., 2011) are commonly used in evaluating text classification.

In recent years, there has been a surge in zeroshot and few-shot approaches to text classification. One approach (Yin et al., 2019, 2020; Halder et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2021) makes use of entailment models. Textual entailment (Dagan et al., 2006), also known as natural language inference (NLI) (Bowman et al., 2015), is the problem of predicting whether a textual premise implies a textual hypothesis in a logical sense. For example, Emma loves apples implies that Emma likes apples. Prompt-based approaches have also been explored extensively, including PET (Schick and Schütze, 2021), LM-BFF (Gao et al., 2021), P-tuning (Liu et al., 2021), prefix-tuning (Li and Liang, 2021), and prompt tuning (Lester et al., 2021).

The entailment approach for text classification sets the input text as the premise and the text repre-senting the label as the hypothesis. A NLI model is applied to each input pair and the entailment probability is used to identify the best matching label. Large-scale NLI resources and models such as SNLI (Bowman et al., 2015), MultiNLI (Williams et al., 2018), ANLI (Nie et al., 2020), XNLI (Conneau et al., 2018), and ESIM/Decomposable Attention baselines (Parikh et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2017) are frequently employed in this line of work.

In this paper, we investigate an alternative based on Siamese Networks (SN) (Bromley et al., 1993), also known as dual encoders. These models embed both input and label texts into a common vector space. The similarity of the two items can then be computed using a similarity function such as the dot product. The advantage is that input and label text are encoded independently, which means that the label embeddings can be pre-computed. Therefore, at inference time, only a single call to the model per input is needed. In contrast, the models typically applied in the entailment approach are Cross Attention (CA) models which need to be executed for every combination of text and label. On the other hand, they allow for interaction between the tokens of label and input, so that in theory they should be superior in classification accuracy. However, in this work we show that in practice, the difference in quality is small. Related dual-encoder and metric-learning methods include SBERT (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019), DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020), ANCE (Xiong et al., 2021), ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020), and USE (Cer et al., 2018).

Both CA and SNs also support the few-shot learning setup by fine-tuning the models on a small number of labeled examples. This is usually done by updating all parameters of the model, which in turn makes it impossible to share the models between different tasks. In this work, we show that when using a SN, one can decide to only fine-tune the label embeddings. We call this Label Tuning (LT). With LT the encoder can be shared between different tasks, which greatly eases the deployment of this approach in a production setup. LT comes with a certain drop in quality, but this drop can be compensated by using a variant of knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2014). Parameter-efficient adaptation techniques have been widely studied, including Adapters (Houlsby et al., 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2020), BitFit (Ben-Zaken et al., 2022), LoRA (Hu et al., 2022), and Compacter (Mahabadi et al., 2021).

Our contributions are as follows: We perform a large study on a diverse set of tasks showing that CA models and SN yield similar performance for both zero-shot and few-shot text classification.  (LT). At training time, input and label texts (hypotheses) are processed by the encoder. LT then tunes the labels using a cross entropy (CE) loss. At inference time, the input text is passed through the same encoder. The tuned label embeddings and a similarity function are then used to score each label. The encoder remains unchanged and can be shared between multiple tasks. Evaluation settings commonly cover GLUE (Wang et al., 2018), SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019), XTREME (Hu et al., 2020), and CLUE (Xu et al., 2020). Similarity and loss choices in dual-encoder literature span cosine or dot-product similarity with cross-entropy, triplet loss (Hoffer and Ailon, 5), contrastive loss (Hadsell et al., 2006), and InfoNCE (Oord et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2020).

In contrast to most prior work, we also show that these results can also be achieved for languages other than English. Multilingual encoders such as mBERT (Devlin et al., 2019), XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020), LaBSE (Feng et al., 2020), and LASER (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019) are commonly used for cross-lingual transfer. We compare the hypothesis patterns commonly used in the literature and using the plain label name (null hypothesis) and find that on average there is no significant difference in performance. Work on prompt calibration and verbalizer design includes contextual calibration (Zhao et al., 2021), automatic verbalizers (Schick and Schütze, 2021), knowledgeable prompting (Liu et al., 2022), and pattern-exploiting training variants (Schick and Schütze, 2021). Finally, we present LT as an alternative to full fine-tuning that allows using the same model for many tasks and thus greatly increases the scalability of the method. We will release the code and trained models used in our experiments. Implementations and resources are available in widely used toolkits such as HuggingFace Transformers (Wolf et al., 2020), Sentence-Transformers (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019), FAISS (Johnson et al., 2017), and Scikit-learn (Pedregosa et al., 2011).

Related Work

There is an important line of work that focuses on claim verification. This includes techniques that predict the veracity of a claim based on the text of the claim only (Rashkin et al., 2017), linguistic features (Popat et al., 2017), meta information about the claimant (e.g., name, job, party affiliation, veracity history) (Wang, 2017b), review articles (Augenstein et al., 2019;Shu et al., 2018;Nakov et al., 2021), as well as relevant articles returned by a search engine (Popat et al., 2018;Augenstein et al., 2019;Mishra and Setty, 2019). To the best of our knowledge, none of the existing work considers the problem of claim verification based on premise articles. There is an important distinction between articles returned by a search engine in previous work and the premise articles that we consider. The techniques that use a search engine to find articles related to a claim query the search engine after a fact checking website has published a review article and therefore end up retrieving articles that include the review article as well as other articles that summarize and/or discuss the verdict of the fact checking website. Hence they are tackling an entailment problem. In contrast, the premise articles that we consider are the source articles used by a fact checker before publishing a review article. Those articles contain relevant facts, but not a summary or discussion of the review article since they are published before the review article and in fact serve as premises for the review article.

Closely related to claim verification is the problem of fake news detection. In this problem, the credibility of an entire news article is evaluated. The credibility of a news article can be estimated based on linguistic and textual features (Conroy et al., 2015;Reis et al., 2019;Li et al., 2019), discourse level structure (Karimi and Tang, 2019), network analysis (Conroy et al., 2015), knowledge graphs (Cui et al., 2020), inter-user behaviour dynamics (Gangireddy et al., 2020) or a combination of multiple modalities . Some techniques reorder the articles returned by a search engine based on their degree of credibility (Olteanu et al., 2013;Beylunioglu, 2020). An important task that can help the detection of fake news is the task of stance detection (Borges et al., 2019;Jwa et al., 2019), i.e., does the content of an article agree or disagree with the title of the article? The following surveys summarize existing work on fake news detection: (Kumar and Shah, 2018;Bondielli and Marcelloni, 2019).

Early fact-checking datasets and benchmarks include FEVER (Thorne et al., 2018), LIAR (Wang, 2017a), FNC-1 (Pomerleau and Rao, 2017), and Hoaxy (Shao et al., 2018). Approaches leveraging external knowledge bases draw on Wikidata, DBpedia, YAGO, and Freebase for evidence alignment (Erxleben et al., 2014;Auer et al., 2007;Suchanek et al., 2007;Bollacker et al., 2008). Neural stance detection methods employ attention-based classifiers, hierarchical encoders, graph networks, and adversarial training (Mohtarami et al., 2018;Sun et al., 2018;Ghosh et al., 2019;Wei and Li, 2019). Rumor verification on social media has been studied with Twitter15, PHEME, RumourEval, and CoAID datasets (Lukasik et al., 2015;Zubiaga et al., 2018;Gorrell et al., 2019;Cui and Lee, 2020). Document retrieval components have incorporated BM25, DPR, ColBERT, and sentence-BERT for evidence selection (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Khattab and Zaharia, 2020;Reimers and Gurevych, 2019). Explainable fact-checking models generate rationales with extractive highlighting, sequence-to-sequence summaries, ERASER-style annotations, and counterfactual augmentations (Lei et al., 2016;DeYoung et al., 2020;Atanasova et al., 2020;Narang et al., 2020). Cross-lingual verification has explored multilingual transformers, translation-based pipelines, multilingual FEVER, and cross-lingual retrieval corpora (Conneau et al., 2020;Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019;Aly et al., 2021;Litschko et al., 2021). Temporal reasoning for fact verification considers event time-lines, time-aware embeddings, temporal knowledge graphs, and diachronic corpora (Zhou et al., 2019;Bianchi et al., 2020;Trivedi et al., 2017;Dhingra et al., 2018). Graph-based methods model claim–evidence relations with heterogeneous graphs, page-level link structures, propagation trees, and knowledge graph paths (Yasunaga et al., 2017;Liu et al., 2020;Kotonya and Toni, 2020;Chen et al., 2019). Pretraining for verification has leveraged Wikipedia, CC-News, OpenWebText, and newswire corpora to enhance evidence retrieval and stance classification (Devlin et al., 2019;Liu et al., 2019;Gururangan et al., 2020;Zellers et al., 2019).

Related Work

Zero-shot cross-lingual structured prediction. Zero-shot cross-lingual learning becomes an emerging research topic as it eliminates the requirement of labeled data for training models in low-resource languages. Multilingual pre-trained encoders widely used for cross-lingual transfer include mBERT (Devlin et al., 2019), XLM (Lample and Conneau, 2019), XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020), and InfoXLM (Chi et al., 2021). Other backbone models such as mT5 (Xue et al., 2021), ByT5 (Xue et al., 2022), RemBERT (Chung et al., 2021), and LaBSE (Feng et al., 2020) have also been explored. Various structured prediction tasks have be studied, including named entity recognition (Pan et al., 2017;Hu et al., 2020), dependency parsing (Ahmad et al., 2019, relation extraction (Zou et al., 2018;Ni and Florian, 2019), and event argument extraction (Subburathinam et al., 2019;Ahmad et al., 2021;Nguyen and Nguyen, 2021). Most of them are classification-based models that build classifiers on top of a multilingual pre-trained masked language models. To further deal with the discrepancy between languages, some of them require additional information, such as bilingual dictionaries (Liu et al., 2019; Ni and Florian, 2019), translation pairs (Zou et al., 2018), and dependency parse trees (Subburathinam et al., 2019;Ahmad et al., 2021;Nguyen and Nguyen, 2021). Cross-lingual alignment techniques cover methods like MUSE (Lample et al., 2018), VecMap (Artetxe et al., 2018), multilingual sentence encoders LASER (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019), and language-agnostic representations like LaBSE (Feng et al., 2020). Common evaluation benchmarks for cross-lingual sequence labeling include CoNLL-2002/2003 (Tjong Kim Sang, 2002; Tjong Kim Sang and De Meulder, 2003), WikiAnn (Pan et al., 2017), CrossNER (Liu et al., 2021), and MultiCoNER (Malmasi et al., 2022). However, as pointed out by previous literature (Li et al., 2021;Hsu et al., 2021), classification-based models are less powerful to model dependencies between entities compared to generation-based models.

Generation-based structured prediction. Several works have demonstrated the great success of generation-based models on monolingual structured prediction tasks, including named entity recognition (Yan et al., 2021), relation extraction (Huang et al., 2021;Paolini et al., 2021), and event extraction (Du et al., 2021;Hsu et al., 2021;Lu et al., 2021). Sequence-to-sequence architectures leveraged for structured prediction often rely on T5 (Raffel et al., 2020), BART (Lewis et al., 2020b), GPT-2-style decoders (Radford et al., 2019), and mT5 (Xue et al., 2021). Yet, as mentioned in Section 1, their designed generating targets are language-dependent. Approaches that linearize target structures include schema-annotated generation (Paolini et al., 2021), sequence labeling as generation for NER (Yan et al., 2021), generative relation extraction (Hwang et al., 2021), and event-centric text generation targets (Du et al., 2021). Accordingly, directly applying their methods to the zero-shot cross-lingual setting would result in less-preferred performance. Constrained decoding and template-based generation have been adopted with pointer or copy mechanisms, including CopyNet (Gu et al., 2016), pointer networks (Vinyals et al., 2015), constrained beam search (Hokamp and Liu, 2017), and lexically constrained decoding (Post and Vilar, 2018).

Prompting methods. There are growing interests recently to incorporate prompts on pre-trained language models in order to guide the models' behavior or elicit knowledge (Shin et al., 2020;Schick and Schütze, 2021;Qin and Eisner, 2021;Scao and Rush, 2021). Representative prompting methods include PET (Schick and Schütze, 2021), LM-BFF (Gao et al., 2021), AutoPrompt (Shin et al., 2020), and P-Tuning (Liu et al., 2021). Following the taxonomy in (Liu et al., 2021), these methods can be classified depending on whether the language models' parameters are tuned and on whether trainable prompts are introduced. Parameter-efficient prompt-based tuning techniques further include Prefix-Tuning (Li and Liang, 2021), Prompt Tuning (Lester et al., 2021), P-Tuning v2 (Liu et al., 2022), and LoRA (Hu et al., 2022). Our method belongs to the category that fixes the prompts and tune the language models' parameters. Despite the flourish of the research in prompting methods, there is only limited attention being put on multilingual tasks (Winata et al., 2021). Multilingual evaluation suites and tasks considered in prompt studies span XNLI (Conneau et al., 2018), XQuAD (Artetxe et al., 2020), MLQA (Lewis et al., 2020a), and TyDi QA (Clark et al., 2020).

Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. Dense retrievers and hybrid pipelines have been explored in DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020), REALM (Guu et al., 2020), ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020), and ANCE (Xiong et al., 2021), alongside sparse methods such as BM25 (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009) and TF-IDF. The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. Efficient indexing and retrieval use FAISS (Johnson et al., 2017), HNSW (Malkov and Yashunin, 2018), ScaNN (Guo et al., 2020), and IVF-PQ (Jegou et al., 2011) for scalable vector search. The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b). Pre-trained encoder-decoder models like T5 (Raffel et al., 2019), BART (Lewis et al., 2019), UL2 (Tay et al., 2022), and mT5 (Xue et al., 2021) have been used as readers across multilingual and zero-shot settings.

Benchmarking ODQA commonly uses datasets such as Natural Questions (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019), TriviaQA (Joshi et al., 2017), WebQuestions (Berant et al., 2013), SQuAD Open (Chen et al., 2017), and HotpotQA (Yang et al., 2018). Training strategies span negative sampling schemes from DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020), in-batch negatives from MoCo (He et al., 2020), hard-negative mining in ANCE (Xiong et al., 2021), and distillation from cross-encoders as in RocketQA (Qu et al., 2021). Evaluation protocols often report Exact Match and F1 on NQ and TriviaQA, utilize recall@k for retrieval as in TREC CAR (Dietz et al., 2017), compute ROUGE-L for generative answers (Lin, 2004), and consider answer calibration metrics like ECE (Naeini et al., 2015). Answer calibration and confidence estimation in ODQA have involved selective prediction (Kamath et al., 2020), conformal prediction (Angelopoulos et al., 2021), entropy-based thresholds (Malinin and Gales, 2018), and self-consistency voting (Wang et al., 2022).

Generative Readers

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework.  and Lewis et al. (2020a) concatenate the given question with top retrieved passages and feed the concatenation to the BART model (Lewis et al., 2019). Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively. Knowledge-augmented generation variants include RAG (Lewis et al., 2020a), ORQA (Lee et al., 2019), FiD (Izacard and Grave, 2020b), and RETRO (Borgeaud et al., 2022), with retrieval components trained on MSMARCO (Nguyen et al., 2016) and Natural Questions (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019). For open-book generation, models draw on Wizard of Wikipedia (Dinan et al., 2019), ELI5 (Fan et al., 2019), MS MARCO Natural Language Generation (Nguyen et al., 2016), and NarrativeQA (Kocisky et al., 2018). Graph-based evidence aggregation appears in GNN readers for multi-hop QA such as GNN-FiD (De Cao et al., 2021), HGN (Fang et al., 2020), GRAFT-Net (Sun et al., 2018), and KGAT (Sun et al., 2019).

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. It has been frequently used in natural language tasks like summarization (Gu et al., 2016;See et al.,  Gehrmann et al., 2018) and neural machine translation (Luong et al., 2014;Gu et al., 2017), but its application to ODQA has been less explored.

Introduction

Few-shot learning is the problem of learning classifiers with only a few training examples. Zero-shot learning (Larochelle et al., 2008), also known as dataless classification (Chang et al., 2008), is the extreme case, in which no labeled data is used. For text data, this is usually accomplished by representing the labels of the task in a textual form, which can either be the name of the label or a concise textual description. Common benchmarks include AG News, DBPedia, Yelp Polarity, Yahoo Answers, and Amazon Reviews (Zhang et al., 2015; McAuley and Leskovec, 2013; Zhang et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2019).

In recent years, there has been a surge in zeroshot and few-shot approaches to text classification. One approach (Yin et al., 2019, 2020; Halder et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2021) makes use of entailment models. Textual entailment (Dagan et al., 2006), also known as natural language inference (NLI) (Bowman et al., 2015), is the problem of predicting whether a textual premise implies a textual hypothesis in a logical sense. For example, Emma loves apples implies that Emma likes apples. Other zero-shot approaches leverage prompt-based tuning and generative language models (Brown et al., 2020; Schick and Schütze, 2021; Gao et al., 2021; Lester et al., 2021). Standard NLI resources include SNLI, MultiNLI, ANLI, and XNLI (Bowman et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2018; Nie et al., 2020; Conneau et al., 2018).

The entailment approach for text classification sets the input text as the premise and the text repre-senting the label as the hypothesis. A NLI model is applied to each input pair and the entailment probability is used to identify the best matching label. Representative cross-encoder architectures comprise ESIM, DeBERTa, RoBERTa-MNLI, and ELECTRA-MNLI (Chen et al., 2017; He et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2019; Clark et al., 2020).

In this paper, we investigate an alternative based on Siamese Networks (SN) (Bromley et al., 1993), also known as dual encoders. Related dual-encoder designs include DSSM, CDSSM, Quick-Thought, and Sentence-BERT (Huang et al., 2013; Shen et al., 2014; Logeswaran and Lee, 2018; Reimers and Gurevych, 2019). These models embed both input and label texts into a common vector space. The similarity of the two items can then be computed using a similarity function such as the dot product. The advantage is that input and label text are encoded independently, which means that the label embeddings can be pre-computed. Therefore, at inference time, only a single call to the model per input is needed. In contrast, the models typically applied in the entailment approach are Cross Attention (CA) models which need to be executed for every combination of text and label. On the other hand, they allow for interaction between the tokens of label and input, so that in theory they should be superior in classification accuracy. However, in this work we show that in practice, the difference in quality is small. Efficiency studies compare bi-encoders and cross-encoders in retrieval and classification (Gillick et al., 2019; Karpukhin et al., 2020; Humeau et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021).

Both CA and SNs also support the few-shot learning setup by fine-tuning the models on a small number of labeled examples. This is usually done by updating all parameters of the model, which in turn makes it impossible to share the models between different tasks. In this work, we show that when using a SN, one can decide to only fine-tune the label embeddings. We call this Label Tuning (LT). With LT the encoder can be shared between different tasks, which greatly eases the deployment of this approach in a production setup. LT comes with a certain drop in quality, but this drop can be compensated by using a variant of knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2014). Parameter-efficient techniques such as adapters, LoRA, prefix tuning, and BitFit have been explored for few-shot transfer (Houlsby et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2022; Li and Liang, 2021; Zaken et al., 2022).

Our contributions are as follows: We perform a large study on a diverse set of tasks showing that CA models and SN yield similar performance for both zero-shot and few-shot text classification.  (LT). At training time, input and label texts (hypotheses) are processed by the encoder. LT then tunes the labels using a cross entropy (CE) loss. At inference time, the input text is passed through the same encoder. The tuned label embeddings and a similarity function are then used to score each label. The encoder remains unchanged and can be shared between multiple tasks. Knowledge distillation and contrastive objectives like KD, PKD, MoCo, and InfoNCE are widely used for representation learning (Hinton et al., 2015; Sun et al., 2019; He et al., 2020; van den Oord et al., 2018).

In contrast to most prior work, we also show that these results can also be achieved for languages other than English. Multilingual evaluations commonly use mBERT, XLM-R, mT5, and XTREME-R (Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021; Ruder et al., 2021). We compare the hypothesis patterns commonly used in the literature and using the plain label name (null hypothesis) and find that on average there is no significant difference in performance. Prompting strategies span manual templates, PET, LM-BFF, and P-tuning v2 (Schick and Schütze, 2021; Gao et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2022; Lester et al., 2021). Finally, we present LT as an alternative to full fine-tuning that allows using the same model for many tasks and thus greatly increases the scalability of the method. We will release the code and trained models used in our experiments.

Related Work

Vision-and-language navigation. Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) requires an agent to find the routes to the desired target based on instructions Thomason et al., 2020;Nguyen and Daumé III, 2019;Chen et al., 2019;Krantz et al., 2020). Recent datasets and protocols for VLN include R2R (Anderson et al., 2018), R4R (Jain et al., 2019), RxR (Ku et al., 2020), CVDN (Thomason et al., 2020), and Touchdown built on Matterport3D (Chen et al., 2019;Chang et al., 2017). Specifically, there are two key challenges in VLN: grounding the natural language instruction to visual environments and generalizing to unseen environments. Common training regimes span behavioral cloning and student-forcing with cross-entropy, on-policy reinforcement learning with A2C or self-critical sequence training, scheduled sampling and DAGGER-style updates (Ma et al., 2019b;Tan et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020b;Ke et al., 2019;Zhu et al., 2020). To address the first challenge, one line of research in VLN utilizes carefully designed cross-modal attention modules (Wang et al., , 2019aTan et al., 2019;Landi et al., 2019;Xia et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2020b,a;Zhu et al., 2020;Zhu et al., 2021;, progress monitor modules (Ma et al., 2019b,a;Ke et al., 2019), and object-action aware modules (Qi et al., 2020a). Another line of research improves vision and language co-grounding by improving vision and language representations with pre-training techniques (Li et al., 2019;Huang et al., 2019b;Hong et al., 2021). Li et al. (2019) directly adopts pre-trained BERT for encoding instructions,  and Hong et al. (2021) learn from a large amount of image-textaction triplets,  learns from large amount of text-image pairs from the web, and Huang et al. (2019b) transfers language and visual representation to in-domain representation with auxiliary tasks. Pre-training objectives vary across masked language modeling, masked region modeling, contrastive InfoNCE and triplet losses with hard negatives (Li et al., 2019;Huang et al., 2019b;Wang et al., 2020a;Zhu et al., 2021). Navigation planners encompass panoramic action abstractions, waypoint selection with learned subgoals, continuous control via low-level velocities, and topological map construction (Wang et al., 2019a;Qi et al., 2020a;Krantz et al., 2020;Xia et al., 2020). Different from them, we utilize the visually-aligned multilingual instructions to learn a cross-lingual language representation that inherently captures visual semantics underlying the instruction.

Multiple methods have been proposed to encourage generalization to unseen environments during training (Zhang et al., 2020;Tan et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020c;Fu et al., 2020). Zhang et al. (2020) demonstrates that it is the low-level appearance information that causes the large performance gap between seen and unseen environments. Tan et al. (2019) proposes to use environment dropout on visual features to create new environments and Fu et al. (2020) utilizes adversarial path sampling to encourage generalization. Data augmentation strategies also include Speaker-Follower generation, back-translation of instructions, EnvDrop perturbations, and paraphrasing with pre-trained language models (Fried et al., 2018;Tan et al., 2019;Li et al., 2019;Ma et al., 2019a;Ke et al., 2019). However, both of these methods rely on a speaker module to generate synthetic training data and can be considered as data augmentation methods, which are complementary to our proposed environment-agnostic visual representation. The closest work to ours is Wang et al. (2020c), where they proposes to pair an environment classifier with gradient reversal layer to learn an environment-agnostic representation. However, they only consider one single environment when learning the visual representation for a given path (i.e., given one path and predict its environment). In our environment-agnostic representation learning, we explore the connections between multiple environments (i.e., maximize the similarity between paths from different environments). Evaluation commonly reports success rate (SR), success weighted by path length (SPL), normalized and soft normalized dynamic time warping (nDTW, sDTW), and path length penalties across benchmarks (Anderson et al., 2018;Ilharco et al., 2019;Jain et al., 2019;Thomason et al., 2020). Vision-and-language with multilinguality. There has been growing interest in combining vision and language for tasks such as visual-guided machine translation (Sigurdsson et al., 2020;Surís et al., 2020;Huang et al., 2020), multi-lingual visual question answering (Gao et al., 2015;Gupta et al., 2020;Shimizu et al., 2018), multi-lingual image captioning (Gu et al., 2018;Lan et al., 2017), multilingual video captioning (Wang et al., 2019b), and multi-lingual image-sentence retrieval Burns et al., 2020). Resources for multilingual alignment and cross-lingual supervision include Multi30K, MUSE bilingual lexicons, WikiMatrix sentence pairs, and CCAligned corpora (Gu et al., 2018;Lan et al., 2017;Sigurdsson et al., 2020;Surís et al., 2020). Architectural choices span LSTM-based sequence encoders with attention, graph attention networks over navigation graphs, multimodal Transformers such as ViLBERT/UNITER, and BERT-style instruction encoders (Tan et al., 2019;Li et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020a;Hong et al., 2021). Training heuristics frequently include curriculum schedules, mixed teacher–student forcing, entropy regularization for exploration, and path abstraction via panoramic discretization (Zhu et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2020b;Ma et al., 2019b;Fu et al., 2020). In this paper, we work on multi-lingual vision-and-language navigation. We use vision (i.e., navigation path) as a bridge between multi-lingual instructions and learn a crosslingual representation that captures visual concepts. Furthermore, our approach also use language as a bridge between different visual environments to learn an environment-agnostic visual representation. Multilingual VLN testbeds and auxiliary resources have included RxR and its language splits, translation-based variants of R2R, multilingual VQA corpora, and cross-lingual image–sentence retrieval datasets (Ku et al., 2020;Gao et al., 2015;Gupta et al., 2020;Shimizu et al., 2018;Burns et al., 2020).

Memory-Augmented Methods

Memory-augmented approaches explore key–value memories, differentiable retrieval, token indexing, and cache-based adapters (Miller et al., 2016; Khandelwal et al., 2020; Grave et al., 2017; Bapna and Firat, 2019).

Related work

Uncertainty estimation in deep learning is a topic that has been studied extensively. Bayesian deep learning includes a family of methods that attempt to capture the notion of uncertainty in deep neural networks. Work on uncertainty-aware calibration and ensembling includes temperature scaling (Guo et al., 2017), deep ensembles (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017), stochastic weight averaging-Gaussian (Maddox et al., 2019), and Laplace approximations for neural networks (Ritter et al., 2018). Applications span datasets such as ImageNet, CIFAR-10, MNIST, and Cityscapes, with evaluation protocols varying between expected calibration error, negative log-likelihood, Brier score, and predictive entropy. Such methods have gained increased popularity in the deep learning literature and there exist multiple applications in subfields such as Computer Vision (Kendall and Gal, 2017;Litjens et al., 2017; and Natural Language Processing (NLP) (Siddhant and Lipton, 2020;Lyu et al., 2020;. In computer vision, semantic segmentation and detection pipelines cite Bayesian SegNet (Kendall et al., 2015), Mask R-CNN (He et al., 2017), Monte Carlo BatchNorm (Teye et al., 2018), and heteroscedastic loss modeling (Kendall and Gal, 2017).

Despite their obvious advantage of modeling uncertainty, the main problem with Bayesian deep learning methods is the computational cost of full Bayesian inference. To tackle this problem, Gal and Ghahramani (2016) propose using standard dropout (Srivastava et al., 2014) as a practical approximation of Bayesian inference in deep neural networks and call this method Monte Carlo dropout. Recent probabilistic sequence models consider variational transformers (Dai et al., 2019), noisy student training (Xie et al., 2020), label smoothing (Szegedy et al., 2016), and stochastic attention mechanisms (Bahdanau et al., 2015; Fortunato et al., 2017).  use a convolutional neural network with Monte Carlo dropout in order to obtain an uncertainty estimate for active learning in the task of image classification. Houlsby et al. (2011) sample many networks with Monte Carlo simulation and propose an objective function that takes into account the disagreement and confidence of the predictions coming from these networks. For active learning in NLP, pool-based selection with BALD (Houlsby et al., 2011), core-set selection (Sener and Savarese, 2018), uncertainty sampling (Lewis and Gale, 1994), and diversity-based criteria (Ash et al., 2020) have been reported on tasks like SNLI and CoNLL-2003.

Similar methods have also been applied to NLP. In machine translation,  extend the Transformer architecture with MC dropout to get a Variational Transformer, and use it to sample multiple translations from the approximate posterior distribution. In machine translation benchmarks, WMT14 En-De, IWSLT14 De-En, WMT16 Ro-En, and WMT19 Zh-En appear frequently alongside BLEU, chrF, and COMET as scoring metrics. They also introduce BLEUVar, an uncertainty metric based on the BLEU score (Papineni et al., 2002) between pairs of the generated translations. Lyu et al. (2020) extend the work of  to question answering and propose an active learning approach based on a modified BLEUVar version. Similarly,  use a conditional random field to obtain uncertainty estimates for active learning and apply their method to named entity recognition. Question answering benchmarks considered include SQuAD, Natural Questions, TriviaQA, and HotpotQA, paired with readers based on BiDAF (Seo et al., 2017), ELECTRA (Clark et al., 2020), RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), and DeBERTa (He et al., 2021). For sequence classification, studies report uncertainty metrics on GLUE, SuperGLUE, Yelp, and AG News, comparing MC dropout, deep ensembles, stochastic depth (Huang et al., 2016), and evidential deep learning (Sensoy et al., 2018).

Although summarization is a prominent NLP task, summarization uncertainty has not been widely studied. Summarization research often references CNN/DailyMail, XSum, Gigaword, and NEWSROOM, with systems like PEGASUS (Zhang et al., 2020), BART (Lewis et al., 2020), T5 (Raffel et al., 2020), and ProphetNet (Qi et al., 2020).  is the only work that focuses on uncertainty for summarization, but their work does not make use of Bayesian methods. They define a generated summary's uncertainty based on the entropy of each token generated by the model during the decoding phase. Their study includes experiments on CNN/DM and XSum using the PEGASUS and BART summarization models. Calibration for text generation has been studied using temperature scaling, Dirichlet prior networks (Malinin and Gales, 2018), conformal prediction (Vovk et al., 2005), and risk-controlled decoding (Malinin et al., 2020). Their main focus is on understanding different properties of uncertainty during the decoding phase, and their work is not directly comparable to ours.

Introduction

Few-shot learning is the problem of learning classifiers with only a few training examples. Zero-shot learning (Larochelle et al., 2008), also known as dataless classification (Chang et al., 2008), is the extreme case, in which no labeled data is used. For text data, this is usually accomplished by representing the labels of the task in a textual form, which can either be the name of the label or a concise textual description. Large pre-trained encoders and decoders have been widely applied to zero- and few-shot classification, including BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), T5 (Raffel et al., 2020), GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020), and XLNet (Yang et al., 2019). Benchmark datasets commonly used for evaluation include AG News (Zhang et al., 2015), DBPedia (Zhang et al., 2015), Yelp Polarity (Zhang et al., 2015), SST-2 (Socher et al., 2013), and Yahoo Answers (Zhang et al., 2015).

In recent years, there has been a surge in zeroshot and few-shot approaches to text classification. One approach (Yin et al., 2019, 2020; Halder et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2021) makes use of entailment models. Textual entailment (Dagan et al., 2006), also known as natural language inference (NLI) (Bowman et al., 2015), is the problem of predicting whether a textual premise implies a textual hypothesis in a logical sense. For example, Emma loves apples implies that Emma likes apples. Beyond entailment models, recent prompt-based approaches adapt pre-trained LMs with task-specific templates and verbalizers (Petroni et al., 2019; Schick and Schütze, 2021; Gao et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021; Lester et al., 2021).

The entailment approach for text classification sets the input text as the premise and the text repre-senting the label as the hypothesis. A NLI model is applied to each input pair and the entailment probability is used to identify the best matching label. Pattern-exploiting training and related methods introduce cloze-style hypotheses or label verbalizers to cast classification as NLI or masked-language modeling (Schick and Schütze, 2021; Yin et al., 2019; Gao et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2021; Jiang et al., 2020).

In this paper, we investigate an alternative based on Siamese Networks (SN) (Bromley et al., 1993), also known as dual encoders. These models embed both input and label texts into a common vector space. The similarity of the two items can then be computed using a similarity function such as the dot product. The advantage is that input and label text are encoded independently, which means that the label embeddings can be pre-computed. Therefore, at inference time, only a single call to the model per input is needed. In contrast, the models typically applied in the entailment approach are Cross Attention (CA) models which need to be executed for every combination of text and label. On the other hand, they allow for interaction between the tokens of label and input, so that in theory they should be superior in classification accuracy. However, in this work we show that in practice, the difference in quality is small. Dual-encoder architectures have been extensively used in retrieval and matching tasks with in-batch negatives and large candidate sets (Henderson et al., 2017; Gillick et al., 2019; Karpukhin et al., 2020; Reimers and Gurevych, 2019; Humeau et al., 2020). Contrastive and metric-learning objectives for representation learning have also been explored in text classification and retrieval contexts (Kiros et al., 2015; Hadsell et al., 2006; Henderson et al., 2017; Gao et al., 2021; Karpukhin et al., 2020).

Both CA and SNs also support the few-shot learning setup by fine-tuning the models on a small number of labeled examples. This is usually done by updating all parameters of the model, which in turn makes it impossible to share the models between different tasks. In this work, we show that when using a SN, one can decide to only fine-tune the label embeddings. We call this Label Tuning (LT). With LT the encoder can be shared between different tasks, which greatly eases the deployment of this approach in a production setup. LT comes with a certain drop in quality, but this drop can be compensated by using a variant of knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2014). Parameter-efficient adaptation techniques such as adapter layers, prefix tuning, and low-rank updates have become common for few-shot scenarios (Houlsby et al., 2019; Li and Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2022; Pfeiffer et al., 2021). Knowledge distillation for compact text classifiers has been studied in various forms, including logit matching and patient distillation (Hinton et al., 2015; Sanh et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2019; Aguilar et al., 2020).

Our contributions are as follows: We perform a large study on a diverse set of tasks showing that CA models and SN yield similar performance for both zero-shot and few-shot text classification. At inference time, the input text is passed through the same encoder. The tuned label embeddings and a similarity function are then used to score each label. The encoder remains unchanged and can be shared between multiple tasks.

In contrast to most prior work, we also show that these results can also be achieved for languages other than English. For multilingual settings, cross-lingual resources and models such as XNLI (Conneau et al., 2018), mBERT (Devlin et al., 2019), XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020), LaBSE (Feng et al., 2020), and LASER (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019) have been used. We compare the hypothesis patterns commonly used in the literature and using the plain label name (null hypothesis) and find that on average there is no significant difference in performance. Evaluation suites for transfer and robustness often consider GLUE (Wang et al., 2018), SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019), CLUE (Xu et al., 2020), and XTREME (Hu et al., 2020). Finally, we present LT as an alternative to full fine-tuning that allows using the same model for many tasks and thus greatly increases the scalability of the method. We will release the code and trained models used in our experiments.

Related Work

Vision-and-language navigation. Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) requires an agent to find the routes to the desired target based on instructions Thomason et al., 2020;Nguyen and Daumé III, 2019;Chen et al., 2019;Krantz et al., 2020). Beyond R2R, several benchmarks expand navigation and instruction complexity, including R4R (Jain et al., 2019), RxR (Ku et al., 2020), CVDN (Thomason et al., 2019), HANNA (Nguyen et al., 2020), and Touchdown (Chen et al., 2019b). Specifically, there are two key challenges in VLN: grounding the natural language instruction to visual environments and generalizing to unseen environments. To address the first challenge, one line of research in VLN utilizes carefully designed cross-modal attention modules (Wang et al., , 2019aTan et al., 2019;Landi et al., 2019;Xia et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2020b,a;Zhu et al., 2020;Zhu et al., 2021;, progress monitor modules (Ma et al., 2019b,a;Ke et al., 2019), and object-action aware modules (Qi et al., 2020a). Attention and fusion variants include co-attention (Lu et al., 2019), bilinear pooling (Kim et al., 2018), gated fusion (Yu et al., 2019), and transformer-based decoders for alignment (Vaswani et al., 2017; Dong et al., 2019). Another line of research improves vision and language co-grounding by improving vision and language representations with pre-training techniques (Li et al., 2019;Huang et al., 2019b;Hong et al., 2021). Large-scale vision-language pretraining has produced models like UNITER (Chen et al., 2020), LXMERT (Tan and Bansal, 2019), OSCAR (Li et al., 2020), VinVL (Zhang et al., 2021), and VisualBERT (Li et al., 2019a). Li et al. (2019) directly adopts pre-trained BERT for encoding instructions,  and Hong et al. (2021) learn from a large amount of image-textaction triplets,  learns from large amount of text-image pairs from the web, and Huang et al. (2019b) transfers language and visual representation to in-domain representation with auxiliary tasks. Multilingual encoders such as mBERT (Devlin et al., 2019), XLM (Lample and Conneau, 2019), XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020), mT5 (Xue et al., 2021), and mBART (Liu et al., 2020) provide cross-lingual representations leveraged in downstream tasks. Different from them, we utilize the visually-aligned multilingual instructions to learn a cross-lingual language representation that inherently captures visual semantics underlying the instruction.

Multiple methods have been proposed to encourage generalization to unseen environments during training (Zhang et al., 2020;Tan et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020c;Fu et al., 2020). Synthetic instruction generation has been studied via speaker models (Fried et al., 2018), back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016), paraphrasing with PEGASUS (Zhang et al., 2020b), and templated recomposition (Thomason et al., 2020b). Zhang et al. (2020) demonstrates that it is the low-level appearance information that causes the large performance gap between seen and unseen environments. Tan et al. (2019) proposes to use environment dropout on visual features to create new environments and Fu et al. (2020) utilizes adversarial path sampling to encourage generalization. Navigation evaluation commonly reports SPL, Success@k, and nDTW (Anderson et al., 2018; Ilharco et al., 2019; Magalhães et al., 2019; Ilharco et al., 2018), with variants for continuous control. However, both of these methods rely on a speaker module to generate synthetic training data and can be considered as data augmentation methods, which are complementary to our proposed environment-agnostic visual representation. Multilingual VLN settings have been explored in RxR with multilingual annotations (Ku et al., 2020), XL-R2R translations (Chen et al., 2021), cross-lingual transfer baselines (Krishna et al., 2021), and instruction normalization pipelines (Mishra et al., 2020). The closest work to ours is Wang et al. (2020c), where they proposes to pair an environment classifier with gradient reversal layer to learn an environment-agnostic representation. Cross-lingual multimodal datasets include Multi30K (Elliott et al., 2016), XVNLI (Gokhale et al., 2020), WIT (Srinivasan et al., 2021), and Reuters Multilingual Visual QA (Liang et al., 2020). However, they only consider one single environment when learning the visual representation for a given path (i.e., given one path and predict its environment). In our environment-agnostic representation learning, we explore the connections between multiple environments (i.e., maximize the similarity between paths from different environments). Vision-and-language with multilinguality. There has been growing interest in combining vision and language for tasks such as visual-guided machine translation (Sigurdsson et al., 2020;Surís et al., 2020;Huang et al., 2020), multi-lingual visual question answering (Gao et al., 2015;Gupta et al., 2020;Shimizu et al., 2018), multi-lingual image captioning (Gu et al., 2018;Lan et al., 2017), multilingual video captioning (Wang et al., 2019b), and multi-lingual image-sentence retrieval Burns et al., 2020). Image-text contrastive training with CLIP (Radford et al., 2021), ALIGN (Jia et al., 2021), LiT (Zhai et al., 2022), SimVLM (Wang et al., 2021), and Florence (Yuan et al., 2021) has been widely explored. In this paper, we work on multi-lingual vision-and-language navigation. We use vision (i.e., navigation path) as a bridge between multi-lingual instructions and learn a crosslingual representation that captures visual concepts. Furthermore, our approach also use language as a bridge between different visual environments to learn an environment-agnostic visual representation. External knowledge grounding resources used in multimodal learning include ConceptNet (Speer et al., 2017), Wikidata (Vrandečić and Krötzsch, 2014), VisualGenome (Krishna et al., 2017), ATOMIC (Sap et al., 2019), and HowTo100M (Miech et al., 2019).

Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Common ODQA benchmarks include Natural Questions (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019), TriviaQA (Joshi et al., 2017), WebQuestions (Berant et al., 2013), and HotpotQA (Yang et al., 2018). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. Dense retrieval methods such as ORQA (Lee et al., 2019), DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020), REALM (Guu et al., 2020), and ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020) are widely adopted. The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. Hard-negative mining strategies include BM25 negatives (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009), in-batch negatives (Henderson et al., 2017), ANCE (Xiong et al., 2021), and RocketQA (Qu et al., 2021). The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Reranking with cross-encoders has been explored with BERT re-ranker (Nogueira and Cho, 2019), MonoT5 (Nogueira et al., 2020), docT5query (Nogueira et al., 2019), and DeepCT (Dai and Callan, 2019). Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b). Retriever evaluation commonly uses MS MARCO (Nguyen et al., 2016), BEIR (Thakur et al., 2021), LoTTE (Santhanam et al., 2021), and TREC Deep Learning (Craswell et al., 2020). Prompting-based ODQA has examined GPT-3 zero-shot and few-shot prompting (Brown et al., 2020), chain-of-thought prompting (Wei et al., 2022), Self-Ask (Press et al., 2022), and ReAct (Yao et al., 2022). Multi-hop retrieval approaches include PathRetriever (Asai et al., 2020), MDR (Xiong et al., 2021), Iterative Retriever-Reader (Qi et al., 2021), and GRAFT-Net (Sun et al., 2018).

Generative Readers

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework. Retrieval-augmented generators include RAG (Lewis et al., 2020a), REALM (Guu et al., 2020), RETRO (Borgeaud et al., 2022), and ATLAS (Izacard et al., 2023).  and Lewis et al. (2020a) concatenate the given question with top retrieved passages and feed the concatenation to the BART model (Lewis et al., 2019). Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively. Closed-book QA baselines have used T5-11B (Roberts et al., 2020), BART-Large (Lewis et al., 2019), UnifiedQA (Khashabi et al., 2020), and GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020).

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. It has been frequently used in natural language tasks like summarization (Gu et al., 2016;See et al.,  Gehrmann et al., 2018) and neural machine translation (Luong et al., 2014;Gu et al., 2017), but its application to ODQA has been less explored. Large-scale QA resources include PAQ (Lewis et al., 2021), AmbigQA (Min et al., 2020), ELI5 (Fan et al., 2019), and MS MARCO QA (Nguyen et al., 2016).

Introduction

Few-shot learning is the problem of learning classifiers with only a few training examples. Zero-shot learning (Larochelle et al., 2008), also known as dataless classification (Chang et al., 2008), is the extreme case, in which no labeled data is used. For text data, this is usually accomplished by representing the labels of the task in a textual form, which can either be the name of the label or a concise textual description.

Standard text classification datasets include AG News, DBPedia, Yelp Polarity, Yahoo Answers, and SST-2 (Zhang et al., 2015; Socher et al., 2013).

Early work on zero-shot text classification explored label embeddings, topic models, and dataless methods on 20 Newsgroups, RCV1, Reuters-21578, and Yahoo Answers (Lampert et al., 2009; Lewis et al., 2004; Chang et al., 2008; Zhang et al., 2015).

In recent years, there has been a surge in zeroshot and few-shot approaches to text classification. One approach (Yin et al., 2019, 2020; Halder et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2021) makes use of entailment models. Textual entailment (Dagan et al., 2006), also known as natural language inference (NLI) (Bowman et al., 2015), is the problem of predicting whether a textual premise implies a textual hypothesis in a logical sense. For example, Emma loves apples implies that Emma likes apples.

Cross-encoder architectures based on BERT, RoBERTa, DeBERTa, and ELECTRA have been widely used for entailment-style classification and ranking (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019; He et al., 2021; Clark et al., 2020).

Prompt-based methods have applied PET, iPET, ADAPET, and EFL with backbones such as RoBERTa, T5, and BART (Schick and Schutze, 2021; Tam et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020).

The entailment approach for text classification sets the input text as the premise and the text repre-senting the label as the hypothesis. A NLI model is applied to each input pair and the entailment probability is used to identify the best matching label.

Calibration methods like temperature scaling, label smoothing, entropy regularization, and focal loss have been examined for zero-shot and few-shot classification (Guo et al., 2017; Muller et al., 2019; Pereyra et al., 2017; Lin et al., 2017).

In this paper, we investigate an alternative based on Siamese Networks (SN) (Bromley et al., 1993), also known as dual encoders. These models embed both input and label texts into a common vector space. The similarity of the two items can then be computed using a similarity function such as the dot product. The advantage is that input and label text are encoded independently, which means that the label embeddings can be pre-computed. Therefore, at inference time, only a single call to the model per input is needed. In contrast, the models typically applied in the entailment approach are Cross Attention (CA) models which need to be executed for every combination of text and label. On the other hand, they allow for interaction between the tokens of label and input, so that in theory they should be superior in classification accuracy. However, in this work we show that in practice, the difference in quality is small.

Representation learning approaches like SBERT, SimCSE, CoSENT, and LaBSE have been evaluated on STS and SentEval as well as retrieval benchmarks (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019; Gao et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2021; Feng et al., 2020; Conneau and Kiela, 2018).

Both CA and SNs also support the few-shot learning setup by fine-tuning the models on a small number of labeled examples. This is usually done by updating all parameters of the model, which in turn makes it impossible to share the models between different tasks. In this work, we show that when using a SN, one can decide to only fine-tune the label embeddings. We call this Label Tuning (LT). With LT the encoder can be shared between different tasks, which greatly eases the deployment of this approach in a production setup. LT comes with a certain drop in quality, but this drop can be compensated by using a variant of knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2014).

Parameter-efficient techniques such as prompt tuning, prefix tuning, P-tuning, and adapters have been studied across GLUE and SuperGLUE with T5 and GPT-3 (Lester et al., 2021; Li and Liang, 2021; Liu et al., 2021; Houlsby et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019; Brown et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020).

Knowledge distillation variants, including teacher-student compression, self-distillation, data augmentation distillation, and task-specific distillers, have been applied to BERT, TinyBERT, and DistilBERT (Hinton et al., 2015; Jiao et al., 2020; Sanh et al., 2019; Furlanello et al., 2018).

Street-level navigation benchmarks couple long instructions with real imagery and map constraints (Chen et al., 2019;de Vries et al., 2018;Mirowski et al., 2019;Hermann et al., 2020). Vision-and-language with multilinguality. There has been growing interest in combining vision and language for tasks such as visual-guided machine translation (Sigurdsson et al., 2020;Surís et al., 2020;Huang et al., 2020), multi-lingual visual question answering (Gao et al., 2015;Gupta et al., 2020;Shimizu et al., 2018), multi-lingual image captioning (Gu et al., 2018;Lan et al., 2017), multilingual video captioning (Wang et al., 2019b), and multi-lingual image-sentence retrieval Burns et al., 2020). Multilingual navigation datasets extend English-only corpora with human translations and new annotations (Ku et al., 2020;Thomason et al., 2020;Chen et al., 2019;Anderson et al., 2018). In this paper, we work on multi-lingual vision-and-language navigation. We use vision (i.e., navigation path) as a bridge between multi-lingual instructions and learn a crosslingual representation that captures visual concepts. Furthermore, our approach also use language as a bridge between different visual environments to learn an environment-agnostic visual representation.

Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Common ODQA benchmarks include Natural Questions, TriviaQA, WebQuestions, and CuratedTREC (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Joshi et al., 2017; Berant et al., 2013; Baudiš and Šedivý, 2015). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. Dense retrieval approaches such as DPR, ORQA, REALM, and ColBERT have been widely adopted to improve first-stage retrieval (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Lee et al., 2019; Guu et al., 2020; Khattab and Zaharia, 2020). The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Training strategies include in-batch negatives, hard negative mining, cross-batch memory, and knowledge distillation for retrievers (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2021; Luan et al., 2021; Izacard and Grave, 2020a). Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. Hybrid retrievers combining sparse and dense signals include DocT5Query, DeepCT, SparTerm, and GAR (Nogueira et al., 2019; Dai and Callan, 2019; Gao et al., 2021; Mao et al., 2021). Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b). Evaluation metrics typically report exact match, F1, hit@k, and nDCG across benchmarks (Rajpurkar et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2017; Karpukhin et al., 2020; Craswell, 2009). Multi-hop QA datasets such as HotpotQA, QAngaroo, 2WikiMultihopQA, and MuSiQue are frequently used to assess reasoning over multiple documents (Yang et al., 2018; Welbl et al., 2018; Ho et al., 2020; Trivedi et al., 2022). Preprocessing pipelines often rely on BM25 indexing, Wikipedia snapshots, hyperlink graphs, and heuristic paragraph segmenters (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009; Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Witten and Milne, 2008; Chen et al., 2017).

Generative Readers

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework.  and Lewis et al. (2020a) concatenate the given question with top retrieved passages and feed the concatenation to the BART model (Lewis et al., 2019). Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively. Knowledge-augmented generators like RAG, FiD, Atlas, and RETRO integrate retrieval with sequence-to-sequence models (Lewis et al., 2020b; Izacard and Grave, 2020b; Izacard et al., 2022; Borgeaud et al., 2022). Answer generation models leverage architectures including BART, T5, PEGASUS, and ProphetNet for sequence modeling (Lewis et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020; Qi et al., 2021). Contrastive pretraining for text encoders has been explored with SimCSE, TSDAE, DeCLUTR, and Contriever in retrieval settings (Gao et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021; Giorgi et al., 2021; Izacard et al., 2021).

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. It has been frequently used in natural language tasks like summarization (Gu et al., 2016;See et al.,  Gehrmann et al., 2018) and neural machine translation (Luong et al., 2014;Gu et al., 2017), but its application to ODQA has been less explored.
