Introduction

Few-shot learning is the problem of learning classifiers with only a few training examples. Zero-shot learning (Larochelle et al., 2008), also known as dataless classification (Chang et al., 2008), is the extreme case, in which no labeled data is used. For text data, this is usually accomplished by representing the labels of the task in a textual form, which can either be the name of the label or a concise textual description. Prior evaluations commonly use benchmarks such as AG News, Yelp Polarity, DBPedia, Amazon Reviews, and SNLI for transfer (Zhang et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2015; Lehmann et al., 2015; McAuley and Leskovec, 2013; Bowman et al., 2015). Prompting-based zero-shot methods include PET, EFL, P-tuning, and LM-BFF (Schick and Schütze, 2021; Wang et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021; Gao et al., 2021).

In recent years, there has been a surge in zeroshot and few-shot approaches to text classification. One approach (Yin et al., 2019, 2020; Halder et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2021) makes use of entailment models. Textual entailment (Dagan et al., 2006), also known as natural language inference (NLI) (Bowman et al., 2015), is the problem of predicting whether a textual premise implies a textual hypothesis in a logical sense. For example, Emma loves apples implies that Emma likes apples. Pre-trained encoders such as BERT, RoBERTa, DeBERTa, and XLNet have been applied (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019; He et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2019). Cross-lingual classification has been studied with XLM-R, mBERT, LASER, and LaBSE on XNLI and PAWS-X (Conneau et al., 2020; Devlin et al., 2019; Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019; Feng et al., 2020; Conneau et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2019).

The entailment approach for text classification sets the input text as the premise and the text repre-senting the label as the hypothesis. A NLI model is applied to each input pair and the entailment probability is used to identify the best matching label. Evaluation protocols vary across GLUE, SuperGLUE, MRPC, and QQP, sometimes including non-classification tasks like SQuAD and CoNLL-03 NER (Wang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019; Dolan and Brockett, 2005; Iyer et al., 2017; Rajpurkar et al., 2016; Tjong Kim Sang and De Meulder, 2003).

In this paper, we investigate an alternative based on Siamese Networks (SN) (Bromley et al., 1993), also known as dual encoders. These models embed both input and label texts into a common vector space. The similarity of the two items can then be computed using a similarity function such as the dot product. The advantage is that input and label text are encoded independently, which means that the label embeddings can be pre-computed. Therefore, at inference time, only a single call to the model per input is needed. In contrast, the models typically applied in the entailment approach are Cross Attention (CA) models which need to be executed for every combination of text and label. On the other hand, they allow for interaction between the tokens of label and input, so that in theory they should be superior in classification accuracy. However, in this work we show that in practice, the difference in quality is small. Dual-encoder retrieval architectures are widely used in DPR, ColBERT, ANCE, and SimCSE for sentence similarity and search (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Khattab and Zaharia, 2020; Xiong et al., 2021; Gao et al., 2021).

Both CA and SNs also support the few-shot learning setup by fine-tuning the models on a small number of labeled examples. This is usually done by updating all parameters of the model, which in turn makes it impossible to share the models between different tasks. In this work, we show that when using a SN, one can decide to only fine-tune the label embeddings. We call this Label Tuning (LT). With LT the encoder can be shared between different tasks, which greatly eases the deployment of this approach in a production setup. LT comes with a certain drop in quality, but this drop can be compensated by using a variant of knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2014). Training strategies encompass contrastive learning, margin-based losses, temperature scaling, and mixup augmentation (Hadsell et al., 2006; Chen et al., 2020; Sohn, 2016; Zhang et al., 2018).

Our contributions are as follows: We perform a large study on a diverse set of tasks showing that CA models and SN yield similar performance for both zero-shot and few-shot text classification.  (LT). At training time, input and label texts (hypotheses) are processed by the encoder. LT then tunes the labels using a cross entropy (CE) loss. At inference time, the input text is passed through the same encoder. The tuned label embeddings and a similarity function are then used to score each label. The encoder remains unchanged and can be shared between multiple tasks. Calibration and thresholding have been explored with temperature scaling, Platt scaling, isotonic regression, and conformal prediction (Guo et al., 2017; Platt, 1999; Zadrozny and Elkan, 2002; Vovk et al., 2005).

In contrast to most prior work, we also show that these results can also be achieved for languages other than English. We compare the hypothesis patterns commonly used in the literature and using the plain label name (null hypothesis) and find that on average there is no significant difference in performance. Finally, we present LT as an alternative to full fine-tuning that allows using the same model for many tasks and thus greatly increases the scalability of the method. We will release the code and trained models used in our experiments. Data augmentation techniques for text include back-translation, EDA, paraphrase generation using PEGASUS, and synthetic labeling with self-training (Sennrich et al., 2016; Wei and Zou, 2019; Zhang et al., 2020; Yarowsky, 1995). Benchmark suites for few-shot evaluation often report on RTE, SST-2, TREC, and SNIPS alongside ImageNet (Bentivogli et al., 2009; Socher et al., 2013; Li and Roth, 2002; Coucke et al., 2018; Deng et al., 2009).

Related Work

Vision-and-language navigation. Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) requires an agent to find the routes to the desired target based on instructions Thomason et al., 2020;Nguyen and Daumé III, 2019;Chen et al., 2019;Krantz et al., 2020). Specifically, there are two key challenges in VLN: grounding the natural language instruction to visual environments and generalizing to unseen environments. Instruction-following datasets such as Room-to-Room (Anderson et al., 2018), Room-Across-Room (Ku et al., 2020), CVDN (Thomason et al., 2020), Touchdown (Chen et al., 2019a), and Talk the Walk (de Vries et al., 2018) provide diverse settings for grounded navigation. To address the first challenge, one line of research in VLN utilizes carefully designed cross-modal attention modules (Wang et al., , 2019aTan et al., 2019;Landi et al., 2019;Xia et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2020b,a;Zhu et al., 2020;Zhu et al., 2021;, progress monitor modules (Ma et al., 2019b,a;Ke et al., 2019), and object-action aware modules (Qi et al., 2020a). Common training strategies include student-forcing and DAGGER variants (Ross et al., 2011;Fang et al., 2019;Laskey et al., 2017;Zhu et al., 2020), as well as speaker-driven data augmentation and back-translation (Fried et al., 2018;Tan et al., 2019;Jain et al., 2019;Hahn et al., 2020). Another line of research improves vision and language co-grounding by improving vision and language representations with pre-training techniques (Li et al., 2019;Huang et al., 2019b;Hong et al., 2021). Representational approaches range from progress monitors and self-monitoring (Ma et al., 2019a;Ma et al., 2019b), to panoramic action spaces (Fried et al., 2018;Anderson et al., 2018), and cross-modal transformers (Hao et al., 2020;Zhou et al., 2020;Lu et al., 2019;Chen et al., 2020). Li et al. (2019) directly adopts pre-trained BERT for encoding instructions,  and Hong et al. (2021) learn from a large amount of image-textaction triplets,  learns from large amount of text-image pairs from the web, and Huang et al. (2019b) transfers language and visual representation to in-domain representation with auxiliary tasks. Multimodal pretraining objectives incorporate masked language modeling, masked region modeling, and contrastive alignment (Devlin et al., 2019;Lu et al., 2019;Chen et al., 2020;Radford et al., 2021), with instruction-conditioned variants for navigation (Huang et al., 2019b;Hong et al., 2021). Different from them, we utilize the visually-aligned multilingual instructions to learn a cross-lingual language representation that inherently captures visual semantics underlying the instruction.

Multiple methods have been proposed to encourage generalization to unseen environments during training (Zhang et al., 2020;Tan et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020c;Fu et al., 2020). Action abstraction has leveraged program induction, options, and macro-actions (Andreas et al., 2017;Jiang et al., 2019;Hu et al., 2019;Shah et al., 2020), with hierarchical policies trained via reinforcement learning (Wang et al., 2019c;Zhang et al., 2021). Zhang et al. (2020) demonstrates that it is the low-level appearance information that causes the large performance gap between seen and unseen environments. Domain adaptation techniques include feature alignment and adversarial invariance (Ganin and Lempitsky, 2015;Tzeng et al., 2017;Long et al., 2015;Liu et al., 2020), as well as style transfer over panoramic views (Zhu et al., 2017;Huang et al., 2018). Tan et al. (2019) proposes to use environment dropout on visual features to create new environments and Fu et al. (2020) utilizes adversarial path sampling to encourage generalization. Evaluation metrics include SPL, nDTW, and sDTW (Anderson et al., 2018;Ilharco et al., 2019;Magalhaes et al., 2020;Pase et al., 2020), alongside human-judged success on path fidelity benchmarks (Gordon et al., 2018;Mehta et al., 2020). However, both of these methods rely on a speaker module to generate synthetic training data and can be considered as data augmentation methods, which are complementary to our proposed environment-agnostic visual representation. The closest work to ours is Wang et al. (2020c), where they proposes to pair an environment classifier with gradient reversal layer to learn an environment-agnostic representation. However, they only consider one single environment when learning the visual representation for a given path (i.e., given one path and predict its environment). In our environment-agnostic representation learning, we explore the connections between multiple environments (i.e., maximize the similarity between paths from different environments). Embodied instruction tasks intersect with vision-and-dialog agents and interactive QA (Das et al., 2017;Gao et al., 2018;Nguyen et al., 2019;Gu et al., 2020), with simulators including Matterport3D, Habitat, and Gibson (Chang et al., 2017;Savva et al., 2019;Xia et al., 2018).

Vision-and-language with multilinguality. There has been growing interest in combining vision and language for tasks such as visual-guided machine translation (Sigurdsson et al., 2020;Surís et al., 2020;Huang et al., 2020), multi-lingual visual question answering (Gao et al., 2015;Gupta et al., 2020;Shimizu et al., 2018), multi-lingual image captioning (Gu et al., 2018;Lan et al., 2017), multilingual video captioning (Wang et al., 2019b), and multi-lingual image-sentence retrieval Burns et al., 2020). Multilingual grounding has been studied in RxR and multilingual R2R (Ku et al., 2020;Chen et al., 2019b), mBERT and XLM-R based encoders (Devlin et al., 2019;Conneau et al., 2020;Liu et al., 2019), and cross-lingual retrieval setups (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019;Feng et al., 2020). In this paper, we work on multi-lingual vision-and-language navigation. Pretraining corpora for grounded language include HowTo100M (Miech et al., 2019), Conceptual Captions (Sharma et al., 2018), CC12M (Changpinyo et al., 2021), and WebImageText collections (Jia et al., 2021), as well as narrated egocentric video (Grauman et al., 2022). We use vision (i.e., navigation path) as a bridge between multi-lingual instructions and learn a crosslingual representation that captures visual concepts. Furthermore, our approach also use language as a bridge between different visual environments to learn an environment-agnostic visual representation.

Related Work

Zero-shot cross-lingual structured prediction. Zero-shot cross-lingual learning becomes an emerging research topic as it eliminates the requirement of labeled data for training models in low-resource languages. Various structured prediction tasks have be studied, including named entity recognition (Pan et al., 2017;Hu et al., 2020), dependency parsing (Ahmad et al., 2019, relation extraction (Zou et al., 2018;Ni and Florian, 2019), and event argument extraction (Subburathinam et al., 2019;Ahmad et al., 2021;Nguyen and Nguyen, 2021). Most of them are classification-based models that build classifiers on top of a multilingual pre-trained masked language models. Common evaluation resources include XTREME, XGLUE, XNLI, WikiAnn, MLQA, and TyDi QA (Hu et al., 2020;Liang et al., 2020;Conneau et al., 2018;Pan et al., 2017;Lewis et al., 2020;Clark et al., 2020). Multilingual pre-trained encoders commonly used include mBERT, XLM, XLM-R, mT5, and InfoXLM (Devlin et al., 2019;Conneau and Lample, 2019;Conneau et al., 2020;Xue et al., 2021;Chi et al., 2021). Tokenization and subword segmentation choices are typically drawn from BPE and SentencePiece variants such as BPE, SentencePiece, BPEmb, and byte-level BPE (Sennrich et al., 2016;Kudo, 2018;Heinzerling and Strube, 2018;Bostrom and Durrett, 2020). Parameter-efficient adaptation techniques applied to multilingual models include adapter layers and their variants such as AdapterFusion and MAD-X (Houlsby et al., 2019;Bapna and Firat, 2019;Pfeiffer et al., 2020;Pfeiffer et al., 2021). Cross-lingual alignment has also been explored via sentence-level representations and parallel data using systems like LASER and LaBSE alongside multilingual encoders (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019;Feng et al., 2020;Reimers and Gurevych, 2019;Pires et al., 2019). Adversarial and invariance-based objectives have been adopted to encourage language-agnostic representations in cross-lingual models (Ganin and Lempitsky, 2015;Chen et al., 2018;Keung et al., 2019;Wu and Dredze, 2019). Data transfer strategies frequently rely on translate-train, translate-test, and annotation projection using parallel data (Mayhew et al., 2017;Ni et al., 2017;Hu et al., 2020;Artetxe et al., 2020). Data augmentation techniques such as back-translation and contextual augmentation are also widely investigated for improving low-resource performance (Sennrich et al., 2016;Fadaee et al., 2017;Edunov et al., 2018;Kobayashi, 2018). To further deal with the discrepancy between languages, some of them require additional information, such as bilingual dictionaries (Liu et al., 2019; Ni and Florian, 2019), translation pairs (Zou et al., 2018), and dependency parse trees (Subburathinam et al., 2019;Ahmad et al., 2021;Nguyen and Nguyen, 2021). However, as pointed out by previous literature (Li et al., 2021;Hsu et al., 2021), classification-based models are less powerful to model dependencies between entities compared to generation-based models.

Generation-based structured prediction. Several works have demonstrated the great success of generation-based models on monolingual structured prediction tasks, including named entity recognition (Yan et al., 2021), relation extraction (Huang et al., 2021;Paolini et al., 2021), and event extraction (Du et al., 2021;Hsu et al., 2021;Lu et al., 2021). Sequence-to-sequence backbones employed across multilingual generation settings commonly include BART, mBART, T5, and mT5 (Lewis et al., 2019;Liu et al., 2020;Raffel et al., 2019;Xue et al., 2021). Yet, as mentioned in Section 1, their designed generating targets are language-dependent. Accordingly, directly applying their methods to the zero-shot cross-lingual setting would result in less-preferred performance.

Prompting methods. There are growing interests recently to incorporate prompts on pre-trained language models in order to guide the models' behavior or elicit knowledge (Shin et al., 2020;Schick and Schütze, 2021;Qin and Eisner, 2021;Scao and Rush, 2021). Prompt- and prefix-based adaptation techniques studied in the literature include prompt tuning, P-tuning, and prefix-tuning with pre-trained language models (Gao et al., 2021;Zhong et al., 2021;Li and Liang, 2021;Lester et al., 2021). Following the taxonomy in (Liu et al., 2021), these methods can be classified depending on whether the language models' parameters are tuned and on whether trainable prompts are introduced. Our method belongs to the category that fixes the prompts and tune the language models' parameters. Despite the flourish of the research in prompting methods, there is only limited attention being put on multilingual tasks (Winata et al., 2021).

Multi-hop settings have been explored with HotpotQA (Yang et al., 2018), QAngaroo (Welbl et al., 2018), ComplexWebQuestions (Talmor and Berant, 2018), and 2WikiMultihopQA (Ho et al., 2020).

Efficient indexing approaches include FAISS (Johnson et al., 2017), ScaNN (Guo et al., 2020), HNSW (Malkov and Yashunin, 2018), and IVF-PQ (Jegou et al., 2011).

Calibration and abstention for ODQA have been studied via selective prediction (Geifman and El-Yaniv, 2017), temperature scaling (Guo et al., 2017), conformal prediction (Vovk et al., 2005), and DUQ (van Amersfoort et al., 2020).

Introduction

Few-shot learning is the problem of learning classifiers with only a few training examples. Zero-shot learning (Larochelle et al., 2008), also known as dataless classification (Chang et al., 2008), is the extreme case, in which no labeled data is used. For text data, this is usually accomplished by representing the labels of the task in a textual form, which can either be the name of the label or a concise textual description. Common benchmark suites include GLUE, SuperGLUE, SentEval, and XTREME (Wang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019; Conneau and Kiela, 2018; Hu et al., 2020). Evaluation tasks in prior zero-shot studies frequently cover AG News, DBPedia, SST-2, and TREC (Zhang et al., 2015; Lehmann et al., 2015; Socher et al., 2013; Voorhees and Tice, 2000).

In recent years, there has been a surge in zeroshot and few-shot approaches to text classification. Beyond entailment-based formulations, prompt-based zero-shot classifiers have been explored using GPT-3, LM-BFF, PET, and Prompt Tuning (Brown et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2021; Schick and Schütze, 2021; Lester et al., 2021). One approach (Yin et al., 2019, 2020; Halder et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2021) makes use of entailment models. Textual entailment (Dagan et al., 2006), also known as natural language inference (NLI) (Bowman et al., 2015), is the problem of predicting whether a textual premise implies a textual hypothesis in a logical sense. For example, Emma loves apples implies that Emma likes apples. NLI training resources such as SNLI, MultiNLI, ANLI, and FEVER-NLI are commonly used for entailment-based classification (Bowman et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2018; Nie et al., 2020; Thorne et al., 2018).

The entailment approach for text classification sets the input text as the premise and the text repre-senting the label as the hypothesis. A NLI model is applied to each input pair and the entailment probability is used to identify the best matching label. Label semantics have been enriched with description templates, verbalizers, pattern mining, and calibration techniques (Gao et al., 2021; Schick and Schütze, 2021; Jiang et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2021).

In this paper, we investigate an alternative based on Siamese Networks (SN) (Bromley et al., 1993), also known as dual encoders. Dual-encoder architectures have been widely used in semantic retrieval, response selection, entity linking, and dense passage retrieval (Gillick et al., 2018; Henderson et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2020; Karpukhin et al., 2020). These models embed both input and label texts into a common vector space. The similarity of the two items can then be computed using a similarity function such as the dot product. The advantage is that input and label text are encoded independently, which means that the label embeddings can be pre-computed. Therefore, at inference time, only a single call to the model per input is needed. In contrast, the models typically applied in the entailment approach are Cross Attention (CA) models which need to be executed for every combination of text and label. CA models are instantiated by BiDAF, ESIM, BERT cross-encoders, and DeBERTa cross-encoders in prior studies (Seo et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2017; Devlin et al., 2019; He et al., 2021). On the other hand, they allow for interaction between the tokens of label and input, so that in theory they should be superior in classification accuracy. However, in this work we show that in practice, the difference in quality is small.

Both CA and SNs also support the few-shot learning setup by fine-tuning the models on a small number of labeled examples. Few-shot fine-tuning strategies include adapters, prefix-tuning, LoRA, and BitFit (Houlsby et al., 2019; Li and Liang, 2021; Hu et al., 2022; Zaken et al., 2022). This is usually done by updating all parameters of the model, which in turn makes it impossible to share the models between different tasks. In this work, we show that when using a SN, one can decide to only fine-tune the label embeddings. We call this Label Tuning (LT). With LT the encoder can be shared between different tasks, which greatly eases the deployment of this approach in a production setup. LT comes with a certain drop in quality, but this drop can be compensated by using a variant of knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2014). Knowledge distillation for text classification has been applied with TinyBERT, PKD, DistilBERT, and MiniLM (Jiao et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2019; Sanh et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020).

Our contributions are as follows: We perform a large study on a diverse set of tasks showing that CA models and SN yield similar performance for both zero-shot and few-shot text classification. At inference time, the input text is passed through the same encoder. The tuned label embeddings and a similarity function are then used to score each label. The encoder remains unchanged and can be shared between multiple tasks.

In contrast to most prior work, we also show that these results can also be achieved for languages other than English. Cross-lingual transfer for classification has been studied on XNLI, MLDoc, PAWS-X, and TyDiQA with multilingual encoders (Conneau et al., 2018; Schwenk and Li, 2018; Yang et al., 2019; Clark et al., 2020). We compare the hypothesis patterns commonly used in the literature and using the plain label name (null hypothesis) and find that on average there is no significant difference in performance. Finally, we present LT as an alternative to full fine-tuning that allows using the same model for many tasks and thus greatly increases the scalability of the method. We will release the code and trained models used in our experiments.

Related Work

Vision-and-language navigation. Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) requires an agent to find the routes to the desired target based on instructions Thomason et al., 2020;Nguyen and Daumé III, 2019;Chen et al., 2019;Krantz et al., 2020). Common VLN benchmarks include R2R (Anderson et al., 2018), R4R (Jain et al., 2019), RxR (Ku et al., 2020), CVDN (Thomason et al., 2020), and Touchdown (Chen et al., 2019). Specifically, there are two key challenges in VLN: grounding the natural language instruction to visual environments and generalizing to unseen environments. Standard evaluation metrics include Success Rate and SPL (Anderson et al., 2018), CLS and SDTW (Jain et al., 2019), nDTW (Ilharco et al., 2019), and Coverage Weighted by Length Score (Shah et al., 2018). To address the first challenge, one line of research in VLN utilizes carefully designed cross-modal attention modules (Wang et al., , 2019aTan et al., 2019;Landi et al., 2019;Xia et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2020b,a;Zhu et al., 2020;Zhu et al., 2021;, progress monitor modules (Ma et al., 2019b,a;Ke et al., 2019), and object-action aware modules (Qi et al., 2020a). General-purpose vision-and-language encoders include LXMERT (Tan and Bansal, 2019), UNITER (Chen et al., 2020), ViLBERT (Lu et al., 2019), OSCAR (Li et al., 2020), CLIP (Radford et al., 2021), and ALIGN (Jia et al., 2021). Another line of research improves vision and language co-grounding by improving vision and language representations with pre-training techniques (Li et al., 2019;Huang et al., 2019b;Hong et al., 2021). Large-scale pre-training corpora comprise Conceptual Captions (Sharma et al., 2018), SBU Captions (Ordonez et al., 2011), COCO Captions (Chen et al., 2015), Visual Genome (Krishna et al., 2017), and WIT (Srinivasan et al., 2021). Li et al. (2019) directly adopts pre-trained BERT for encoding instructions,  and Hong et al. (2021) learn from a large amount of image-textaction triplets,  learns from large amount of text-image pairs from the web, and Huang et al. (2019b) transfers language and visual representation to in-domain representation with auxiliary tasks. Navigation policies and training strategies span sequence-to-sequence followers (Anderson et al., 2018), Speaker-Follower augmentation (Fried et al., 2018), Reinforced Cross-Modal learning (Wang et al., 2019b), and Self-Monitoring agents (Ma et al., 2019a). Architectures with stronger spatial reasoning include PREVALENT (Hao et al., 2020), VLN-BERT (Majumdar et al., 2020), HAMT (Chen et al., 2021), and Airbert (Guhur et al., 2021). Different from them, we utilize the visually-aligned multilingual instructions to learn a cross-lingual language representation that inherently captures visual semantics underlying the instruction.

Multiple methods have been proposed to encourage generalization to unseen environments during training (Zhang et al., 2020;Tan et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020c;Fu et al., 2020). Augmentation and regularization techniques include back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016), Easy Data Augmentation (Wei and Zou, 2019), domain randomization (Tobin et al., 2017), mixup (Zhang et al., 2018), and CutMix (Yun et al., 2019). Zhang et al. (2020) demonstrates that it is the low-level appearance information that causes the large performance gap between seen and unseen environments. Tan et al. (2019) proposes to use environment dropout on visual features to create new environments and Fu et al. (2020) utilizes adversarial path sampling to encourage generalization. However, both of these methods rely on a speaker module to generate synthetic training data and can be considered as data augmentation methods, which are complementary to our proposed environment-agnostic visual representation. The closest work to ours is Wang et al. (2020c), where they proposes to pair an environment classifier with gradient reversal layer to learn an environment-agnostic representation. However, they only consider one single environment when learning the visual representation for a given path (i.e., given one path and predict its environment). In our environment-agnostic representation learning, we explore the connections between multiple environments (i.e., maximize the similarity between paths from different environments). Vision-and-language with multilinguality. There has been growing interest in combining vision and language for tasks such as visual-guided machine translation (Sigurdsson et al., 2020;Surís et al., 2020;Huang et al., 2020), multi-lingual visual question answering (Gao et al., 2015;Gupta et al., 2020;Shimizu et al., 2018), multi-lingual image captioning (Gu et al., 2018;Lan et al., 2017), multilingual video captioning (Wang et al., 2019b), and multi-lingual image-sentence retrieval Burns et al., 2020). Multilingual text encoders widely used across tasks include mBERT (Devlin et al., 2019), XLM (Lample and Conneau, 2019), XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020), mT5 (Xue et al., 2021), LaBSE (Feng et al., 2020), and LASER (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019). Multilingual vision-language resources include Multi30K (Elliott et al., 2016), COCO-CN (Li et al., 2019), WIT (Srinivasan et al., 2021), MaRVL (Liu et al., 2021), and xGQA (Pfeiffer et al., 2022). Video-language pre-training has drawn on corpora and models such as HowTo100M (Miech et al., 2019), VATEX (Wang et al., 2019b), TVC (Lei et al., 2020), HERO (Li et al., 2020b), and Frozen-in-Time (Bain et al., 2021). In this paper, we work on multi-lingual vision-and-language navigation. We use vision (i.e., navigation path) as a bridge between multi-lingual instructions and learn a crosslingual representation that captures visual concepts. Furthermore, our approach also use language as a bridge between different visual environments to learn an environment-agnostic visual representation.

Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Benchmarks such as Natural Questions, WebQuestions, TriviaQA, and MS MARCO are widely used (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Berant et al., 2013; Joshi et al., 2017; Nguyen et al., 2016). Multi-hop QA benchmarks like HotpotQA, QAngaroo, ComplexWebQuestions, and 2WikiMultihopQA are standard for evaluating reasoning (Yang et al., 2018; Welbl et al., 2018; Talmor and Berant, 2018; Ho et al., 2020). Dense and sparse retrievers including BM25, DPR, ANCE, and ColBERT have been evaluated extensively (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009; Karpukhin et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2021; Khattab and Zaharia, 2020). Reranking methods such as monoBERT, T5 re-rankers, RocketQAv2, and doc2query-T5 are also commonly adopted (Nogueira and Cho, 2019; Nogueira et al., 2020; Qu et al., 2021; Nogueira et al., 2019). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b). Common evaluation settings report EM and F1 on NQ-Open, TriviaQA, and WebQuestions using Wikipedia corpora from 2016 and 2020 snapshots (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Joshi et al., 2017; Berant et al., 2013; Petroni et al., 2021). Objectives including maximum marginal likelihood, contrastive loss, knowledge distillation, and denoising pre-training are commonly explored (Smith and Eisner, 2006; Hinton et al., 2015; Xiong et al., 2021; Lewis et al., 2019).

Generative Readers

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework. Generative readers are studied on NQ-Open, TriviaQA, WebQuestions, and CuratedTREC (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Joshi et al., 2017; Berant et al., 2013; Baudiš and Šedivý, 2015). Approaches based on BART, T5, UniLM, and ProphetNet have been compared for abstractive answer generation (Lewis et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2020; Dong et al., 2019; Qi et al., 2020).  and Lewis et al. (2020a) concatenate the given question with top retrieved passages and feed the concatenation to the BART model (Lewis et al., 2019). Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively. Knowledge-augmented models incorporate Fusion-in-Decoder, RAG, REALM, and Atlas for retrieval-augmented generation (Izacard and Grave, 2020b; Lewis et al., 2020a; Guu et al., 2020; Izacard et al., 2022).

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. Copy mechanisms such as CopyNet, pointer sentinel, hybrid pointer-generator, and pointer networks have been used across tasks (Gu et al., 2016; Merity et al., 2016; See et al., 2017; Vinyals et al., 2015). It has been frequently used in natural language tasks like summarization (Gu et al., 2016;See et al.,  Gehrmann et al., 2018) and neural machine translation (Luong et al., 2014;Gu et al., 2017), but its application to ODQA has been less explored.

Introduction

Few-shot learning is the problem of learning classifiers with only a few training examples. Zero-shot learning (Larochelle et al., 2008), also known as dataless classification (Chang et al., 2008), is the extreme case, in which no labeled data is used. For text data, this is usually accomplished by representing the labels of the task in a textual form, which can either be the name of the label or a concise textual description. Early dataless classification leveraged topic models and semantic representations (Chang et al., 2008; Song and Roth, 2014; Maas et al., 2011; Chen et al., 2015), with later methods exploring label embeddings and label descriptions (Nam et al., 2016; Rios and Kavuluru, 2018; Johnson and Zhang, 2017; Zhang et al., 2021).

In recent years, there has been a surge in zeroshot and few-shot approaches to text classification. One approach (Yin et al., 2019, 2020; Halder et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2021) makes use of entailment models. Textual entailment (Dagan et al., 2006), also known as natural language inference (NLI) (Bowman et al., 2015), is the problem of predicting whether a textual premise implies a textual hypothesis in a logical sense. For example, Emma loves apples implies that Emma likes apples. Beyond NLI, prompt-based classification with pre-trained language models has been explored using PET, LM-BFF, and pattern-verbalizer pairs (Schick and Schütze, 2021; Gao et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021; Logan IV et al., 2021).

The entailment approach for text classification sets the input text as the premise and the text repre-senting the label as the hypothesis. A NLI model is applied to each input pair and the entailment probability is used to identify the best matching label.

In this paper, we investigate an alternative based on Siamese Networks (SN) (Bromley et al., 1993), also known as dual encoders. These models embed both input and label texts into a common vector space. The similarity of the two items can then be computed using a similarity function such as the dot product. The advantage is that input and label text are encoded independently, which means that the label embeddings can be pre-computed. Therefore, at inference time, only a single call to the model per input is needed. In contrast, the models typically applied in the entailment approach are Cross Attention (CA) models which need to be executed for every combination of text and label. On the other hand, they allow for interaction between the tokens of label and input, so that in theory they should be superior in classification accuracy. Dual-encoder retrieval architectures such as DPR, ANCE, and ColBERT have also informed text matching strategies (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2020; Khattab and Zaharia, 2020; Hofstätter et al., 2021). However, in this work we show that in practice, the difference in quality is small. Meta-learning approaches like MAML, ProtoNets, Matching Networks, and Reptile are frequently used baselines for few-shot classification (Finn et al., 2017; Snell et al., 2017; Vinyals et al., 2016; Nichol et al., 2018).

Both CA and SNs also support the few-shot learning setup by fine-tuning the models on a small number of labeled examples. This is usually done by updating all parameters of the model, which in turn makes it impossible to share the models between different tasks. In this work, we show that when using a SN, one can decide to only fine-tune the label embeddings. We call this Label Tuning (LT). With LT the encoder can be shared between different tasks, which greatly eases the deployment of this approach in a production setup. LT comes with a certain drop in quality, but this drop can be compensated by using a variant of knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2014). Distillation techniques include teacher-student training, patient knowledge distillation, TinyBERT, and self-distillation (Hinton et al., 2015; Sun et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2020). Parameter-efficient tuning methods like adapters, LoRA, prefix-tuning, and BitFit have been studied for low-resource settings (Houlsby et al., 2019; Li and Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021; Zaken et al., 2022).

Our contributions are as follows: We perform a large study on a diverse set of tasks showing that CA models and SN yield similar performance for both zero-shot and few-shot text classification. Related Work

There is an important line of work that focuses on claim verification. This includes techniques that predict the veracity of a claim based on the text of the claim only (Rashkin et al., 2017), linguistic features (Popat et al., 2017), meta information about the claimant (e.g., name, job, party affiliation, veracity history) (Wang, 2017b), review articles (Augenstein et al., 2019;Shu et al., 2018;Nakov et al., 2021), as well as relevant articles returned by a search engine (Popat et al., 2018;Augenstein et al., 2019;Mishra and Setty, 2019). To the best of our knowledge, none of the existing work considers the problem of claim verification based on premise articles. There is an important distinction between articles returned by a search engine in previous work and the premise articles that we consider. The techniques that use a search engine to find articles related to a claim query the search engine after a fact checking website has published a review article and therefore end up retrieving articles that include the review article as well as other articles that summarize and/or discuss the verdict of the fact checking website. Hence they are tackling an entailment problem. In contrast, the premise articles that we consider are the source articles used by a fact checker before publishing a review article. Those articles contain relevant facts, but not a summary or discussion of the review article since they are published before the review article and in fact serve as premises for the review article.

Commonly used fact-checking datasets include FEVER (Thorne et al., 2018), LIAR (Wang, 2017b), MultiFC (Augenstein et al., 2019), Fakeddit (Nakamura et al., 2020), PHEME (Zubiaga et al., 2016), and GossipCop/PolitiFact collections (Shu et al., 2020).

Evidence retrieval approaches span sparse and dense methods, including TF-IDF/BM25 pipelines (Chen et al., 2017), ORQA (Lee et al., 2019), DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020), ANCE (Xiong et al., 2021), and ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020).

Neural architectures adapted to claim verification include decomposable attention (Parikh et al., 2016), ESIM (Chen et al., 2017), BERT variants (Devlin et al., 2019;Liu et al., 2019;He et al., 2021), and evidential aggregation mechanisms (Hanselowski et al., 2018).

Graph-based fact verification has explored reasoning over knowledge graphs and document graphs (Nie et al., 2019;Zhou et al., 2019;Liu et al., 2020;Yasunaga et al., 2021;Hu et al., 2020).

Explanation-oriented methods provide rationales or highlights for decisions using erasure, attention, and extractive spans (Lei et al., 2016;Jain and Wallace, 2019;DeYoung et al., 2020;Atanasova et al., 2020;Pruthi et al., 2020).

Closely related to claim verification is the problem of fake news detection. In this problem, the credibility of an entire news article is evaluated. The credibility of a news article can be estimated based on linguistic and textual features (Conroy et al., 2015;Reis et al., 2019;Li et al., 2019), discourse level structure (Karimi and Tang, 2019), network analysis (Conroy et al., 2015), knowledge graphs (Cui et al., 2020), inter-user behaviour dynamics (Gangireddy et al., 2020) or a combination of multiple modalities . Some techniques reorder the articles returned by a search engine based on their degree of credibility (Olteanu et al., 2013;Beylunioglu, 2020). An important task that can help the detection of fake news is the task of stance detection (Borges et al., 2019;Jwa et al., 2019), i.e., does the content of an article agree or disagree with the title of the article? The following surveys summarize existing work on fake news detection: (Kumar and Shah, 2018;Bondielli and Marcelloni, 2019).

Stance detection resources include the Fake News Challenge (FNC-1) (Pomerleau and Rao, 2017), RumourEval/SemEval (Derczynski et al., 2017;Gorrell et al., 2019), PHEME (Zubiaga et al., 2016), and Emergent (Ferreira and Vlachos, 2016).

Multimodal misinformation detection leverages text and images with models such as EANN (Wang et al., 2018), SAFE (Zhou et al., 2019), MVAE (Khattar et al., 2019), SpotFake (Singh et al., 2020), and datasets like Weibo and Twitter (Jin et al., 2017;Jin et al., 2020).

Propagation- and user-centric signals have been modeled using temporal dynamics and network structures (Ma et al., 2016;Vosoughi et al., 2018;Jin et al., 2016;Kwon et al., 2017;Wu and Liu, 2019).

Cross-lingual and multilingual fact-checking has been investigated with shared tasks and datasets such as CheckThat! at CLEF (Barron-Cedeno et al., 2020;Shaar et al., 2020), multilingual claim verification corpora (Aly et al., 2021;Gupta et al., 2021), and transfer learning baselines (Conneau et al., 2020;Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019).

Domain adaptation and temporal robustness for misinformation detection have been studied through cross-topic, cross-platform, and time-aware evaluations (Horne et al., 2019;Wadden et al., 2020;Muller et al., 2021;Rottger et al., 2021;Song et al., 2021).

Related Work

Zero-shot cross-lingual structured prediction. Zero-shot cross-lingual learning becomes an emerging research topic as it eliminates the requirement of labeled data for training models in low-resource languages. Various structured prediction tasks have be studied, including named entity recognition (Pan et al., 2017;Hu et al., 2020), dependency parsing (Ahmad et al., 2019, relation extraction (Zou et al., 2018;Ni and Florian, 2019), and event argument extraction (Subburathinam et al., 2019;Ahmad et al., 2021;Nguyen and Nguyen, 2021). Beyond these tasks, cross-lingual POS tagging, semantic role labeling, coreference resolution, and slot filling have also been investigated (Rahimi et al., 2019;Akbik et al., 2015;Fei et al., 2020;Upadhyay et al., 2018). Most of them are classification-based models that build classifiers on top of a multilingual pre-trained masked language models. Multilingual pre-trained encoders widely used in these settings include mBERT, XLM, XLM-R, and InfoXLM (Devlin et al., 2019;Lample and Conneau, 2019;Conneau et al., 2020;Chi et al., 2021). To further deal with the discrepancy between languages, some of them require additional information, such as bilingual dictionaries (Liu et al., 2019; Ni and Florian, 2019), translation pairs (Zou et al., 2018), and dependency parse trees (Subburathinam et al., 2019;Ahmad et al., 2021;Nguyen and Nguyen, 2021). Cross-lingual transfer strategies such as translate-train, translate-test, self-training, and back-translation have been explored (Artetxe et al., 2020;Hu et al., 2020;Xie et al., 2018;Conneau et al., 2020). Alignment techniques based on bilingual lexicon induction, sentence-level mapping, domain-adversarial learning, and contrastive objectives are also common (Lample et al., 2018;Artetxe et al., 2018;Ganin et al., 2016;Feng et al., 2020). Resources frequently used for cross-lingual supervision include MUSE, PanLex, OPUS, and Wikipedia interlanguage links (Lample et al., 2018;Kamholz et al., 2014;Tiedemann, 2012;Smith et al., 2017). However, as pointed out by previous literature (Li et al., 2021;Hsu et al., 2021), classification-based models are less powerful to model dependencies between entities compared to generation-based models.

Generation-based structured prediction. Several works have demonstrated the great success of generation-based models on monolingual structured prediction tasks, including named entity recognition (Yan et al., 2021), relation extraction (Huang et al., 2021;Paolini et al., 2021), and event extraction (Du et al., 2021;Hsu et al., 2021;Lu et al., 2021). Generative formulations for structured prediction span seq2seq tagging, pointer-network extraction, template filling, and text-to-structured conversion (Zeng et al., 2018;Paolini et al., 2021;Rongali et al., 2020;Cai and Lam, 2020). Benchmarks commonly used in these studies include CoNLL-2003, ACE 2005, TACRED, and FewRel (Sang and De Meulder, 2003;Walker et al., 2006;Zhang and Zhang, 2017;Han et al., 2018). Yet, as mentioned in Section 1, their designed generating targets are language-dependent. Accordingly, directly applying their methods to the zero-shot cross-lingual setting would result in less-preferred performance.

Prompting methods. There are growing interests recently to incorporate prompts on pre-trained language models in order to guide the models' behavior or elicit knowledge (Shin et al., 2020;Schick and Schütze, 2021;Qin and Eisner, 2021;Scao and Rush, 2021). Parameter-efficient prompting variants include prefix-tuning, prompt tuning, adapters, and LoRA (Li and Liang, 2021;Lester et al., 2021;Houlsby et al., 2019;Hu et al., 2022). Design choices around verbalizers, calibration, and automatic prompt search have been widely explored (Jiang et al., 2020;Gao et al., 2021;Zhao et al., 2021;Zhong et al., 2021). Following the taxonomy in (Liu et al., 2021), these methods can be classified depending on whether the language models' parameters are tuned and on whether trainable prompts are introduced. Our method belongs to the category that fixes the prompts and tune the language models' parameters. Despite the flourish of the research in prompting methods, there is only limited attention being put on multilingual tasks (Winata et al., 2021). Multilingual prompt-based learning has been examined with mT5, MAD-X, mBART, and RemBERT (Xue et al., 2021;Pfeiffer et al., 2020;Liu et al., 2020;Chung et al., 2021).

Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. Subsequent retriever-reader systems include ORQA (Lee et al., 2019), REALM (Guu et al., 2020), DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020), RAG (Lewis et al., 2020a), and Atlas (Izacard et al., 2022). The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. Dense retrieval methods such as DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020), ANCE (Xiong et al., 2021), ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020), TAS-B (Hofstatter et al., 2021), and Contriever (Izacard et al., 2022) have been widely adopted. Common retrieval corpora include Wikipedia dumps, MS MARCO (Nguyen et al., 2016), C4 (Raffel et al., 2020), KILT (Petroni et al., 2021), and CCNet (Wenzek et al., 2020). The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Common ODQA benchmarks comprise SQuAD Open (Rajpurkar et al., 2016), Natural Questions (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019), TriviaQA (Joshi et al., 2017), WebQuestions (Berant et al., 2013), and CuratedTREC (Baudis and Sedivy, 2015). Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. For multi-hop QA, methods like HotpotQA baselines (Yang et al., 2018), PathRetriever (Asai et al., 2020), GNN-based readers (De Cao et al., 2019), Cognitive Graphs (Song et al., 2018), and MDR (Xiong et al., 2021) have been explored. Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b). Evaluation metrics for ODQA and generation encompass EM/F1 (Rajpurkar et al., 2016), BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002), ROUGE (Lin, 2004), NDCG (Jarvelin and Kekalainen, 2002), and Recall@k (Voorhees, 1999).

Generative Readers

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework. Generative readers include BART (Lewis et al., 2019), T5 (Raffel et al., 2020), RAG (Lewis et al., 2020a), FiD (Izacard and Grave, 2020b), and Atlas (Izacard et al., 2022).  and Lewis et al. (2020a) concatenate the given question with top retrieved passages and feed the concatenation to the BART model (Lewis et al., 2019). Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively. Data augmentation strategies such as back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016), self-training (He et al., 2020), retrieval augmentation (Lewis et al., 2020a), knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2015), and passage expansion with doc2query (Nogueira et al., 2019) are commonly used.

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. Copy-augmented sequence models include CopyNet (Gu et al., 2016), Pointer Sentinel Mixture (Merity et al., 2017), hybrid pointer-generator (See et al., 2017), and pointer networks (Vinyals et al., 2015) applied across tasks. At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. It has been frequently used in natural language tasks like summarization (Gu et al., 2016;See et al.,  Gehrmann et al., 2018) and neural machine translation (Luong et al., 2014;Gu et al., 2017), but its application to ODQA has been less explored. Summarization datasets and models frequently used with pointer mechanisms include CNN/DailyMail (Hermann et al., 2015), Gigaword (Rush et al., 2015), XSum (Narayan et al., 2018), PEGASUS (Zhang et al., 2020), and BART (Lewis et al., 2019).

Introduction

Few-shot learning is the problem of learning classifiers with only a few training examples. Zero-shot learning (Larochelle et al., 2008), also known as dataless classification (Chang et al., 2008), is the extreme case, in which no labeled data is used. For text data, this is usually accomplished by representing the labels of the task in a textual form, which can either be the name of the label or a concise textual description. Benchmarks commonly used for text classification include AG News (Zhang et al., 2015), Yahoo Answers (Zhang et al., 2015), DBPedia (Lehmann et al., 2015), and Yelp Polarity (Zhang et al., 2015).

In recent years, there has been a surge in zeroshot and few-shot approaches to text classification. One approach (Yin et al., 2019, 2020; Halder et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2021) makes use of entailment models. Textual entailment (Dagan et al., 2006), also known as natural language inference (NLI) (Bowman et al., 2015), is the problem of predicting whether a textual premise implies a textual hypothesis in a logical sense. For example, Emma loves apples implies that Emma likes apples. Prompt-based zero-shot methods have explored PET (Schick and Schütze, 2021a, 2021b), LM-BFF (Gao et al., 2021), P-Tuning (Liu et al., 2021a), and Prefix-Tuning (Li and Liang, 2021). Large language models have been studied for zero-shot classification with GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020), T0 (Sanh et al., 2021), FLAN (Wei et al., 2022), and OPT (Zhang et al., 2022).

The entailment approach for text classification sets the input text as the premise and the text repre-senting the label as the hypothesis. A NLI model is applied to each input pair and the entailment probability is used to identify the best matching label. NLI resources frequently used in training and evaluation include SNLI (Bowman et al., 2015), MNLI (Williams et al., 2018), RTE (Bentivogli et al., 2009), and ANLI (Nie et al., 2020).

In this paper, we investigate an alternative based on Siamese Networks (SN) (Bromley et al., 1993), also known as dual encoders. These models embed both input and label texts into a common vector space. The similarity of the two items can then be computed using a similarity function such as the dot product. The advantage is that input and label text are encoded independently, which means that the label embeddings can be pre-computed. Therefore, at inference time, only a single call to the model per input is needed. In contrast, the models typically applied in the entailment approach are Cross Attention (CA) models which need to be executed for every combination of text and label. On the other hand, they allow for interaction between the tokens of label and input, so that in theory they should be superior in classification accuracy. However, in this work we show that in practice, the difference in quality is small. Pretrained encoders commonly adopted include BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), DeBERTa (He et al., 2021), and ELECTRA (Clark et al., 2020). Dual-encoder retrieval and similarity models such as DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020), ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020), ANCE (Xiong et al., 2021), and Contriever (Izacard et al., 2022) have also been applied to related semantic matching problems.

Both CA and SNs also support the few-shot learning setup by fine-tuning the models on a small number of labeled examples. This is usually done by updating all parameters of the model, which in turn makes it impossible to share the models between different tasks. In this work, we show that when using a SN, one can decide to only fine-tune the label embeddings. We call this Label Tuning (LT). With LT the encoder can be shared between different tasks, which greatly eases the deployment of this approach in a production setup. LT comes with a certain drop in quality, but this drop can be compensated by using a variant of knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2014). Parameter-efficient tuning methods include Houlsby adapters (Houlsby et al., 2019), Pfeiffer adapters (Pfeiffer et al., 2021), LoRA (Hu et al., 2022), and BitFit (Ben-Zaken et al., 2022). Calibration techniques for few-shot settings include temperature scaling (Guo et al., 2017), contextualized calibration (Zhao et al., 2021), label smoothing (Szegedy et al., 2016), and distribution alignment (Zhang et al., 2021).

Our contributions are as follows: We perform a large study on a diverse set of tasks showing that CA models and SN yield similar performance for both zero-shot and few-shot text classification. At inference time, the input text is passed through the same encoder. The tuned label embeddings and a similarity function are then used to score each label. The encoder remains unchanged and can be shared between multiple tasks.

In contrast to most prior work, we also show that these results can also be achieved for languages other than English. We compare the hypothesis patterns commonly used in the literature and using the plain label name (null hypothesis) and find that on average there is no significant difference in performance. Finally, we present LT as an alternative to full fine-tuning that allows using the same model for many tasks and thus greatly increases the scalability of the method. We will release the code and trained models used in our experiments. Cross-lingual transfer has been investigated with mBERT (Devlin et al., 2019), XLM (Lample and Conneau, 2019), XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020), and LaBSE (Feng et al., 2020). Evaluation protocols vary across studies, including macro-F1 on TREC (Voorhees and Tice, 2000), accuracy on SST-2 (Socher et al., 2013), micro-F1 on Reuters-21578 (Lewis, 1997), and exact match on SciTail (Khot et al., 2018).

Related Work

Vision-and-language navigation. Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) requires an agent to find the routes to the desired target based on instructions Thomason et al., 2020;Nguyen and Daumé III, 2019;Chen et al., 2019;Krantz et al., 2020). Specifically, there are two key challenges in VLN: grounding the natural language instruction to visual environments and generalizing to unseen environments. Standard VLN datasets include R2R (Anderson et al., 2018), R4R (Jain et al., 2019), RxR (Ku et al., 2020), and CVDN (Thomason et al., 2020). To address the first challenge, one line of research in VLN utilizes carefully designed cross-modal attention modules (Wang et al., , 2019aTan et al., 2019;Landi et al., 2019;Xia et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2020b,a;Zhu et al., 2020;Zhu et al., 2021;, progress monitor modules (Ma et al., 2019b,a;Ke et al., 2019), and object-action aware modules (Qi et al., 2020a). Another line of research improves vision and language co-grounding by improving vision and language representations with pre-training techniques (Li et al., 2019;Huang et al., 2019b;Hong et al., 2021). Pretrained vision-language models widely used for instruction grounding encompass LXMERT (Tan and Bansal, 2019), ViLBERT (Lu et al., 2019), CLIP (Radford et al., 2021), and ALBEF (Li et al., 2021). Li et al. (2019) directly adopts pre-trained BERT for encoding instructions,  and Hong et al. (2021) learn from a large amount of image-textaction triplets,  learns from large amount of text-image pairs from the web, and Huang et al. (2019b) transfers language and visual representation to in-domain representation with auxiliary tasks. Large-scale image–text corpora used for pretraining include Conceptual Captions (Sharma et al., 2018), WIT (Srinivasan et al., 2021), LAION-5B (Schuhmann et al., 2022), and YFCC100M (Thomee et al., 2016). Contrastive objectives for cross-modal alignment include InfoNCE (van den Oord et al., 2018), SimCLR (Chen et al., 2020), MoCo (He et al., 2020), and CLIP-style losses (Radford et al., 2021). Different from them, we utilize the visually-aligned multilingual instructions to learn a cross-lingual language representation that inherently captures visual semantics underlying the instruction.

Multiple methods have been proposed to encourage generalization to unseen environments during training (Zhang et al., 2020;Tan et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020c;Fu et al., 2020). Data augmentation and speaker-based training have been studied with Speaker-Follower (Fried et al., 2018), EnvDrop (Tan et al., 2019), Auxiliary Reasoning (Ma et al., 2019a), and adversarial path sampling (Fu et al., 2020). Zhang et al. (2020) demonstrates that it is the low-level appearance information that causes the large performance gap between seen and unseen environments. Tan et al. (2019) proposes to use environment dropout on visual features to create new environments and Fu et al. (2020) utilizes adversarial path sampling to encourage generalization. However, both of these methods rely on a speaker module to generate synthetic training data and can be considered as data augmentation methods, which are complementary to our proposed environment-agnostic visual representation. The closest work to ours is Wang et al. (2020c), where they proposes to pair an environment classifier with gradient reversal layer to learn an environment-agnostic representation. Domain adaptation techniques relevant to environment generalization include gradient reversal (Ganin and Lempitsky, 2015), adversarial discriminative adaptation (Tzeng et al., 2017), domain randomization (Tobin et al., 2017), and test-time entropy minimization (Grandvalet and Bengio, 2005). However, they only consider one single environment when learning the visual representation for a given path (i.e., given one path and predict its environment). Object-centric grounding leverages region features from Faster R-CNN (Ren et al., 2015), Mask R-CNN (He et al., 2017), Visual Genome annotations (Krishna et al., 2017), and Open Images (Kuznetsova et al., 2020). In our environment-agnostic representation learning, we explore the connections between multiple environments (i.e., maximize the similarity between paths from different environments). Map-augmented navigation has incorporated topological graphs (Chen et al., 2019;Ma et al., 2019b), panoramic action spaces (Anderson et al., 2018;Fried et al., 2018), and semantic maps (Chaplot et al., 2020;Huang et al., 2019b). Evaluation protocols commonly report Success, SPL (Anderson et al., 2018), nDTW (Ilharco et al., 2019), and CLS (Jain et al., 2019). Vision-and-language with multilinguality. There has been growing interest in combining vision and language for tasks such as visual-guided machine translation (Sigurdsson et al., 2020;Surís et al., 2020;Huang et al., 2020), multi-lingual visual question answering (Gao et al., 2015;Gupta et al., 2020;Shimizu et al., 2018), multi-lingual image captioning (Gu et al., 2018;Lan et al., 2017), multilingual video captioning (Wang et al., 2019b), and multi-lingual image-sentence retrieval Burns et al., 2020). Multilingual cross-modal pretraining has explored models such as M3P (Ni et al., 2021), UC2 (Zhou et al., 2021), MURAL (Jain et al., 2021), and x-VLM (Zeng et al., 2022). In this paper, we work on multi-lingual vision-and-language navigation. We use vision (i.e., navigation path) as a bridge between multi-lingual instructions and learn a crosslingual representation that captures visual concepts. Furthermore, our approach also use language as a bridge between different visual environments to learn an environment-agnostic visual representation.

Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b). For dense retrieval, a line of work investigates bi-encoder and dual-encoder models with in-batch negatives and hard negatives (Lee et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Xiong et al., 2021;Qu et al., 2021). Benchmark datasets such as Natural Questions, TriviaQA, WebQuestions, and CuratedTREC are commonly used to evaluate ODQA systems (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019;Joshi et al., 2017;Berant et al., 2013;Baudis and Sedivy, 2015). Hard negative mining strategies include BM25 negatives, cross-batch negatives, and adversarial sampling (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009;Xiong et al., 2021;Qu et al., 2021;Zhan et al., 2021). Knowledge sources range from Wikipedia dumps to web-scale corpora and structured KBs like Freebase and Wikidata (Chen et al., 2017;Bollacker et al., 2008;Vrandevic and Krotzsch, 2014;Petroni et al., 2019).

Generative Readers

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework.  and Lewis et al. (2020a) concatenate the given question with top retrieved passages and feed the concatenation to the BART model (Lewis et al., 2019). Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively. Sequence-to-sequence readers leverage architectures such as BART, T5, UniLM, and PEGASUS for answer generation (Lewis et al., 2019;Raffel et al., 2019;Dong et al., 2019;Zhang et al., 2020). Evidence aggregation techniques include reranking, multi-passage fusion, and late interaction mechanisms (Nogueira and Cho, 2019;Izacard and Grave, 2020b;Khattab and Zaharia, 2020;Lin et al., 2021). Training objectives vary from maximum likelihood to denoising and knowledge distillation (Vaswani et al., 2017;Lewis et al., 2020a;Hinton et al., 2015;Sanh et al., 2019).

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. It has been frequently used in natural language tasks like summarization (Gu et al., 2016;See et al.,  Gehrmann et al., 2018) and neural machine translation (Luong et al., 2014;Gu et al., 2017), but its application to ODQA has been less explored. Copy-augmented models have been explored in dialogue, data-to-text, and question generation (See et al., 2017;Gu et al., 2016;Wiseman et al., 2017;Du et al., 2017). Coverage mechanisms and repetition penalties are commonly combined with pointer networks to mitigate duplication (Tu et al., 2016;See et al., 2017;Paulus et al., 2018;Fan et al., 2018). Evaluation protocols often report Exact Match and F1 on standard benchmarks along with ablations on retriever top-k, reader capacity, and decoding strategies (Rajpurkar et al., 2016;Chen et al., 2017;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Lewis et al., 2020a).

Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. Dense and sparse retrievers include DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020), BM25 (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009), REALM (Guu et al., 2020), ORQA (Lee et al., 2019), ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020), and ANCE (Xiong et al., 2021). The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Commonly used ODQA benchmarks comprise Natural Questions (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019), TriviaQA (Joshi et al., 2017), WebQuestions (Berant et al., 2013), SQuAD Open (Chen et al., 2017), CuratedTREC (Baudiš and Šedivý, 2015), and HotpotQA (Yang et al., 2018). Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b). Retrieval training practices frequently involve in-batch negatives (Henderson et al., 2017), hard negative mining with BM25 (Karpukhin et al., 2020), late interaction using multi-vector representations (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020), and query-side iterative updates as in ANCE (Xiong et al., 2021). Multi-hop question answering approaches include PathRetriever (Asai et al., 2020), GraphRetriever (Min et al., 2019), MDR (Xiong et al., 2021), CogQA (Ding et al., 2019), and datasets such as HotpotQA (Yang et al., 2018) and QAngaroo WikiHop (Welbl et al., 2018). Evaluation and retrieval metrics commonly reported for ODQA include exact match and F1 (Rajpurkar et al., 2016), Hits@k (Johnson et al., 2019), mean reciprocal rank (Craswell, 2009), and nDCG (Järvelin and Kekäläinen, 2002).

Generative Readers

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework.  and Lewis et al. (2020a) concatenate the given question with top retrieved passages and feed the concatenation to the BART model (Lewis et al., 2019). Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively. Retrieval-augmented generators such as RAG (Lewis et al., 2020b), FiD (Izacard and Grave, 2020b), Atlas (Izacard et al., 2022), and RETRO (Borgeaud et al., 2022) instantiate different retrieval and fusion strategies. Decoding strategies employed by generative readers include beam search (Sutskever et al., 2014), nucleus sampling (Holtzman et al., 2019), diverse beam search (Vijayakumar et al., 2016), and constrained decoding with lexical constraints (Hokamp and Liu, 2017). Variations of retrieval-augmented models include RAG-Sequence and RAG-Token (Lewis et al., 2020b), REALM (Guu et al., 2020), RETRO (Borgeaud et al., 2022), and KILT for unified evaluation (Petroni et al., 2021).

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. It has been frequently used in natural language tasks like summarization (Gu et al., 2016;See et al.,  Gehrmann et al., 2018) and neural machine translation (Luong et al., 2014;Gu et al., 2017), but its application to ODQA has been less explored. Pointer-based and copy-augmented architectures have been explored in summarization and translation, including pointer-generator networks (See et al., 2017), copy mechanisms for NMT (Gu et al., 2016; Gulcehre et al., 2016), reinforcement learning for abstractive summarization (Paulus et al., 2018), and long-document summarization on arXiv/PubMed (Cohan et al., 2018). Widely used summarization datasets include CNN/DailyMail (Hermann et al., 2015; Nallapati et al., 2016), Gigaword (Graff et al., 2003), XSum (Narayan et al., 2018), Newsroom (Grusky et al., 2018), PubMed (Cohan et al., 2018), and arXiv (Cohan et al., 2018).

Introduction

Few-shot learning is the problem of learning classifiers with only a few training examples. Zero-shot learning (Larochelle et al., 2008), also known as dataless classification (Chang et al., 2008), is the extreme case, in which no labeled data is used. For text data, this is usually accomplished by representing the labels of the task in a textual form, which can either be the name of the label or a concise textual description.

Pre-trained encoders widely used for zero-shot classification include BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), DeBERTa (He et al., 2021), ELECTRA (Clark et al., 2020), and T5 (Raffel et al., 2020).
Common benchmarks for text classification include SST-2 (Socher et al., 2013), AG News (Zhang et al., 2015), Yahoo Answers (Zhang et al., 2015), DBPedia (Lehmann et al., 2015), and Yelp Reviews (Zhang et al., 2015).

In recent years, there has been a surge in zeroshot and few-shot approaches to text classification. One approach (Yin et al., 2019, 2020; Halder et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2021) makes use of entailment models. Textual entailment (Dagan et al., 2006), also known as natural language inference (NLI) (Bowman et al., 2015), is the problem of predicting whether a textual premise implies a textual hypothesis in a logical sense. For example, Emma loves apples implies that Emma likes apples.

NLI resources such as SNLI (Bowman et al., 2015), MultiNLI (Williams et al., 2018), ANLI (Nie et al., 2020), and XNLI (Conneau et al., 2018) are widely adopted for evaluation and transfer.

The entailment approach for text classification sets the input text as the premise and the text representing the label as the hypothesis. A NLI model is applied to each input pair and the entailment probability is used to identify the best matching label.

Dual-encoder sentence embedding models such as InferSent (Conneau et al., 2017), Universal Sentence Encoder (Cer et al., 2018), Sentence-BERT (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019), and LaBSE (Feng et al., 2020) have been widely used for similarity-based retrieval and classification.

In this paper, we investigate an alternative based on Siamese Networks (SN) (Bromley et al., 1993), also known as dual encoders. These models embed both input and label texts into a common vector space. The similarity of the two items can then be computed using a similarity function such as the dot product. The advantage is that input and label text are encoded independently, which means that the label embeddings can be pre-computed. Therefore, at inference time, only a single call to the model per input is needed. In contrast, the models typically applied in the entailment approach are Cross Attention (CA) models which need to be executed for every combination of text and label. On the other hand, they allow for interaction between the tokens of label and input, so that in theory they should be superior in classification accuracy. However, in this work we show that in practice, the difference in quality is small.

Prompt-based classification has been explored with PET and iPET (Schick and Schütze, 2021a; Schick and Schütze, 2021b), P-Tuning (Liu et al., 2021), Prefix-Tuning (Li and Liang, 2021), and Prompt Tuning (Lester et al., 2021).
Large language models have been studied for in-context learning and few-shot classification with GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020), FLAN (Wei et al., 2022), T0 (Sanh et al., 2022), and instruction tuning methods (Ouyang et al., 2022; Chung et al., 2022).

Both CA and SNs also support the few-shot learning setup by fine-tuning the models on a small number of labeled examples. This is usually done by updating all parameters of the model, which in turn makes it impossible to share the models between different tasks. In this work, we show that when using a SN, one can decide to only fine-tune the label embeddings. We call this Label Tuning (LT). With LT the encoder can be shared between different tasks, which greatly eases the deployment of this approach in a production setup. LT comes with a certain drop in quality, but this drop can be compensated by using a variant of knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2014).

Knowledge distillation for language models has been explored with DistilBERT (Sanh et al., 2019), TinyBERT (Jiao et al., 2020), PKD (Sun et al., 2019), and MobileBERT (Sun et al., 2020).

Our contributions are as follows: We perform a large study on a diverse set of tasks showing that CA models and SN yield similar performance for both zero-shot and few-shot text classification. At inference time, the input text is passed through the same encoder. The tuned label embeddings and a similarity function are then used to score each label. The encoder remains unchanged and can be shared between multiple tasks.

Few-shot learning techniques for NLP also include prototypical networks (Snell et al., 2017), MAML (Finn et al., 2017), Reptile (Nichol et al., 2018), and Meta-Learner LSTM (Ravi and Larochelle, 2017).
Data augmentation strategies for low-resource text classification include back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016), EDA (Wei and Zou, 2019), MixUp (Zhang et al., 2018), and paraphrase generation with PPDB or ParaNMT (Ganitkevitch et al., 2013; Wieting and Gimpel, 2018).

In contrast to most prior work, we also show that these results can also be achieved for languages other than English. We compare the hypothesis patterns commonly used in the literature and using the plain label name (null hypothesis) and find that on average there is no significant difference in performance. Finally, we present LT as an alternative to full fine-tuning that allows using the same model for many tasks and thus greatly increases the scalability of the method. We will release the code and trained models used in our experiments.

Cross-lingual transfer has been studied with mBERT (Devlin et al., 2019), XLM (Lample and Conneau, 2019), XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020), LASER (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019), and LaBSE (Feng et al., 2020).
